coredata.dc:title: Application of carbon-based quantum dots in photodynamic therapy
coredata.dc:creator: Karagianni, Alexandra
Tsierkezos, Nikos G.
Prato, Maurizio
Terrones, Mauricio
Kordatos, Konstantinos V.
coredata.dc:description: Photodynamic Therapy (PDT) is a non-invasive therapeutic modality that can treat a wide variety of cancer types by means of photosensitizer drug, light, and oxygen. Due to enhanced specificity and fewer side effects, PDT can be an alternative approach for cancer treatments. However, conventional photosensitizers (PSs) exhibit low selectivity, hydrophobicity, and limited photophysical properties. Nanotechnology emerges as a potential solution to these issues and improves PDT efficiency. Nanomaterials such as Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) and Graphene Quantum Dots (GrQDs) have been widely applied on PDT research recently, regarding their excellent photoluminescence properties, biocompatibility, as well as their hydrophilicity. The present review article summarizes the main features of PDT and carbon-based quantum dots with an emphasis on used PSs and methods for synthesis of carbon dots. Additionally, the most recent applications of CQDs and GrQDs in PDT have been extensively discussed. The main conclusion that arises is that carbon-based quantum dots seem to be a powerful tool in cancer diagnosis and treatment.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photodynamic Therapy (PDT) is a non-invasive therapeutic modality that can treat a wide variety of cancer types by means of photosensitizer drug, light, and oxygen. Due to enhanced specificity and fewer side effects, PDT can be an alternative approach for cancer treatments. However, conventional photosensitizers (PSs) exhibit low selectivity, hydrophobicity, and limited photophysical properties. Nanotechnology emerges as a potential solution to these issues and improves PDT efficiency. Nanomaterials such as Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) and Graphene Quantum Dots (GrQDs) have been widely applied on PDT research recently, regarding their excellent photoluminescence properties, biocompatibility, as well as their hydrophilicity. The present review article summarizes the main features of PDT and carbon-based quantum dots with an emphasis on used PSs and methods for synthesis of carbon dots. Additionally, the most recent applications of CQDs and GrQDs in PDT have been extensively discussed. The main conclusion that arises is that carbon-based quantum dots seem to be a powerful tool in cancer diagnosis and treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer constitutes a major cause of death worldwide, with estimated 10 million deaths in 2020 [1]. Since there are more than 100 different types of cancer, the treatment appears to be more difficult regarding the danger of metastasis and mutation rate [2,3]. The most common treatment procedures that applied for cancer cure are chemotherapy, radiotherapy, immunotherapy, and surgery (see Fig. 1 ). All these treatments have been extensively used up to now, despite their disadvantages. In particular, chemotherapy is based on using of highly toxic drugs, and consequently patients suffer from severe side effects. Radiotherapy lacks the desired specificity in cancer cells, because radiation can destroy healthy tissues. Furthermore, immunotherapy may trigger the immune system to damage healthy cells, and surgery cannot secure the removal of the whole tumor [3]. It is therefore required to examine some other treatments against cancer, such as photodynamic therapy (PDT) (see Fig. 1).

PDT is a non-invasive therapeutic method that aims to damage cells and tissues. The major point of this medical approach is to produce singlet oxygen by combining photosensitizer drug and irradiation of light. The main advantages of this method are the successful treatment of early-stage cancer, the improvement of patients’ quality of life, and the prolonged survival of patients with inoperable tumors. It is of greatest importance that PDT can secure minimum systemic toxicity compared to other therapies due to the controllable time and point of irradiation. Some limitations of PDT are the hypoxic tumor environment and the depth and location of the tissue. In addition to this, PDT is not completely successful in metastasis and there is a concern about skin photosensitization that forces patients to avoid exposure to light until the residual drug is excluded [4–6]. Nowadays, researchers focus on the development of photosensitizers (PSs) that are non-toxic, localized to target tissue, and capable of producing the desired singlet oxygen.

Nanotechnology has contributed to PDT research by optimizing the existing PSs. Nanomaterials can act as PSs itself or in conjugation with used PSs or even as delivery systems for chemotherapeutic drugs such as doxorubicin (DOX) [4]. A major advantage of nanomaterials is their increased selectivity in tumor tissue compared to normal ones due to their enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect. The EPR effect refers to pathophysiological conditions in solid tumors, such as overexpression of inflammatory factors, inefficient drainage of the lymphatic system, and structural abnormalities in tumor blood vessels. All these characteristics allow the invasion of nanoparticles into tumor cells [7].

One ideal candidate for the contribution of nanotechnology to PDT is the Carbon-Based Quantum Dots. Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) and Graphene Quantum Dots (GrQDs) constitute two representatives of carbon-based Quantum Dots (see Fig. 2 ). These nanomaterials area new kind of carbon-based structure, are zero-dimensional (0D), and exhibit differences regarding their structure and properties [8]. CQDs possess crystalline or amorphous structure, whereas GrQDs are composed of graphene sheet layers and therefore they have the structure of graphene lattice [9]. In particular, the CQDs' structure contains a spherical carbon nucleus and surface functional groups, such as for instance, hydroxyl, carbonyl, and epoxy groups [9–11]. In contrast, GrQDs have layers of graphene with surface functional groups and interlayer defects with larger lateral dimensions compared with their height [12]. The functional groups are highly important for carbon dots’ properties, such as water solubility and photoluminescence and give them the potential to be conjugated with other molecules [9,10]. Both CQDs and GrQDs have carbon atoms with sp2 bonding and show the Quantum Confinement Effect, that affects their photoluminescence properties [9]. The quantum confinement effect in GrQDs is influenced not only by their size, but also by the confinement of conjugated π-domains [13].

Carbon-based quantum dots exhibit great optical and electronic properties, biocompatibility, cost-effective ways of synthesis and thus they can replace the Semiconductor Quantum Dots (SQDs), that are based on toxic heavy metals, and their use in biological fields is debated due to toxicity issues. In contrast, carbon dots can be applied in various biological applications such as bioimaging, drug delivery, and ion detection [9,15]. Regarding their application in PDT, carbon-based quantum dots can optimize the treatment efficiency with their excellent photoluminescence properties, low cytotoxicity, and water solubility, which are lacking in most PSs [16]. The use of carbon dots in PDT is a new research field and has recently stimulated research interest with the fabrication of carbon dots with various precursors and the development of nano-platforms capable of targeting tumor cells and producing singlet oxygen.

This review presents the main principles of PDT, examining the basic components of this therapeutic modality with an emphasis on PSs. Some common techniques for synthesis of CQDs and GrQDs and their properties for PDT applications are also discussed. In addition, the most recent applications of carbon-based quantum dots in PDT for various types of cancer are also displayed in order to give a new perspective on the application of nanomaterials in cancer therapy and diagnosis.

PDT is a therapeutic modality for neoplastic and non-neoplastic diseases, and it can be used alone or in combination with other treatments [4]. PDT is utilized in medical fields such as dermatology, ophthalmology, pulmonology, and urology. Regarding cancer treatment, PDT has treated lesions in the skin, lung, esophagus, prostate, and brain [4,17]. Clinically, actinic keratoses, macular degeneration, atherosclerosis, rheumatoid arthritis, Bowen's disease, peptic ulcer disease, periodontitis, and bacterial infections have been treated by using PDT [4,17–20].

PDT is a procedure that takes place in two stages. Firstly, a substance called photosensitizer (PS) is administered to patients, and afterwards tumors are irradiated with light (see Fig. 3 ). It must be mentioned that only the irradiated areas will be affected, regardless of PS's binding to normal cells. The tendency of PS to accumulate in cancer tissues is explained by the prolific rates of neoplastic cells or the secretion of vascular endothelial growth factors. Another explanation could be the enhanced expression of low density lipoproteins (LDL) in malignant cells. These protein receptors interact with PS and increase PS's concentration in cancer tissues [4,5].

Photophysical and photochemical reactions are conducted during PDT treatment (see Fig. 4 ). Primarily, PS in its ground state (PS) is excited in a singlet state (1PS*) with the absorption of light. The excited state is unstable and PS will fall to the ground state by releasing radiation such as fluorescence or heat. A different route refers to the transformation of the PS from singlet to triplet state (3PS*), which has a longer lifetime. This process is known as intersystem crossing, and it is really important for PDT. The fall to the ground state can be achieved through the release of radiation such as phosphorescence or heat. In the triplet state, PS participates in photochemical reactions with cellular substrates to produce reactive oxygen species (ROS) such as H2O2, OH− or singlet oxygen (1O2). The production of ROS is known as Type I Reactions and requires the presence of molecular oxygen (3O2), in order to form free radicals via electron transfer. Type II Reactions refer to the energy transfer of molecular oxygen to generate singlet oxygen. The products of both types are vital for the destruction of cancer cells, and the contribution of each reaction type depends on the PS [4,21].

The therapeutic outcome arises from three mechanisms: apoptosis, necrosis, and autophagy (see Fig. 5 ). Namely, apoptosis is a multi-step planned cell death process, that results in a series of morphological and biochemical changes. Some of the morphological cell changes reported in apoptosis are chromatin condensation, chromosomal DNA fragmentation, cell shrinkage, and the generation of apoptotic bodies. In terms of biochemistry, a family of proteases named caspases is activated and cytochrome c is released, leading to changes in the cellular substrate. These proteases act as catalysts in hydrolytic reactions. Apoptosis is activated by cell surface death receptors such as “death-inducing signaling complexes” (DISCs). Apoptosis can also be triggered by several factors, including chemicals, radiation, or heat. The role of mitochondria is vital in the apoptosis process, as apoptosis-related proteins are released through this organelle. PDT activates apoptosis with the destruction of anti-apoptotic proteins, such as Bcl-2 family proteins. On the other hand, necrosis refers to accidental cell death, that occurs as a result of pathological insults or low energy levels due to adenosine triphosphate (ATP) depletion. The pyknotic nucleus, cytoplasmic swelling, and gradual disintegration of the plasma membrane are the main morphological characteristics of this unprogrammed cellular death mechanism. In necrosis, pro-inflammotory molecules and intracellular contents are released, leading to an inflammatory reaction. At the biochemical level, DNA fragmentation, release of cytochrome c, and absence of caspase activation describe the process of necrosis. Higher concentrations of PS and light are required in order to achieve necrosis. This leads to considering apoptosis as a preferable cell death mechanism due to its higher efficiency. Autophagy concludes in cell death with the destruction of the cell's organelles. The role of this mechanism is to preserve the balance between the generation, destruction, and recycling of cellular products. In autophagy, a double-membrane structure named the autophagosome is formed, which traps the contents of the desired cell region in a vesicle and transports them to the lysosome. Then, an autophagolysosome is formed, that is degraded by lysosomal hydrolases. Autophagy can also provide nutrients in vital processes from unnecessary ones through this path in cases of starvation or stress. PDT may activate this path by the production of ROS, and autophagy does not depend on the organelle-target of PS, such as mitochondria, endoplasmic reticulum, or lysosomes. Generally, the type of cell, PS, and light dose mainly affect the mechanism of cell death [4,22,23].

Since this review focuses on the contribution of PDT to cancer, it is necessary to investigate in detail the anticancer action of PDT (see Fig. 6 ). One anticancer mechanism is the direct destruction of cancer cells, that is based on apoptosis, necrosis, and autophagy. An alternative approach is vascular damage with the destruction of blood cells due to ROS production that leads to an insufficient supply of nutrients in cancer cells. Tumor growth is delayed as a result of thrombus formulation, vasoconstriction, and necrosis caused by PDT. In contrast to other cancer treatments, PDT can lead to immunity, which is proved by the increase of leukocytes in cancer tissue and overexpression of heat shock proteins [24].

A PS must be eligible in some circumstances in order to be used with safety and efficacy. In particular, it is important for the PS to be selective. This specification ensures that the drug will be accumulated mainly in tumor cells. It is, however, probable to improve PS's selectivity further by binding PSs to molecular delivery systems. These systems have high affinity for cancer tissue, and consequently the irradiation will be focused on a specified volume. The success of the therapy is highly related to the absorption peak of PS. Namely, PS drug must absorb in the wavelength range of 600–800 nm. This constitutes the “optical window” for PDT, where tissue penetration is high and sufficient energy for singlet oxygen generation is secured [25]. Furthermore, PS must be non-toxic, inexpensive and easily producible, and stable in storage. It is of the utmost importance to rapidly clear the PS from the body in order to avoid the problem of photosensitivity. For this purpose, PS must exhibit no dark toxicity. Resistance to photobleaching is a required PS feature so that the PS is not destroyed during treatment. A desirable characteristic is the fluorescence of PS, because PDT can be used as a diagnostic tool and the cancer tissue can be visualized protecting the normal one. Finally, PS should not be a mutagen and should not inhibit other treatment such as chemotherapy or surgery [4,5,17,25,26].

According to their properties and research development, PSs are classified into three main generations. The first generation of PSs includes Photofrin, also known as porfimer sodium, and other water-soluble hematoporphyrin derivatives (see Fig. 7 ). PSs in this generation have a complex structure, that makes the synthesis process quite difficult regarding cost and time-consuming. Except for this, these agents exhibit low light absorption rate at 630 nm, resulting in limited penetration into deeper tissue. Long-lasting skin photosensitivity constitutes the most important side effect. Considering all the above, there is an urgent need to use a developed category of PSs [4,17].

The second generation of PSs includes porphyrins, chlorins, bacteriochlorins, phthalocyanines, and metallo-phthalocyanines (see Fig. 7). Most of them have a cyclic tetrapyrrolic structure. In dermatology, 5-Aminolevulinic acid (ALA) is widely used, which is a biological precursor of protoporphyrin IX (PPIX). In this category, PSs are pure compounds and less toxic than the PSs of the previous generation. They also present satisfactory absorbance in 650–800 nm range. This kind of drug exhibits limited selectivity of target tissue, poor solubility, and penetration depth [17].

The third generation of PSs is highly related to nanotechnology and gene engineering. This category is the latest developed in the field of PDT. The main goal of this new generation is to enhance the selectivity of PSs by developing drug delivery and solubility. In parallel, drug bioavailability is achieved with the use of 3rd generation PSs. Nanoparticles represent this generation and can be used as PSs or they can deliver PSs by conjugation with antibodies or peptides. Alternatively, PSs can be encapsulated in liposomes, in gold nanoparticles, in biodegradable polymer-based nanoparticles or quantum dots and therefore they achieve high selectivity and sensitivity [4,17].

Light sources are a major factor in this therapy since they determine the penetration depth and the selected PS (see Fig. 8 ). However, it must be mentioned that the accepted range of wavelengths for examining tissues is between 600 and 1200 nm. In particular, light with lower wavelengths has the maximum absorbance and enhanced energy according to Planck's Quantum Theory (E = hc/λ). The lower accepted value of 600 nm is determined since endogenous molecules, such as hemoglobin, absorb light at lower values of wavelength [5]. However, at this wavelength, insufficient depth of penetration is observed. This depth is useful in the treatment of skin lesions. Otherwise, it is preferable to use higher wavelengths. Considering the importance of producing singlet oxygen, the maximum accepted wavelength is 800 nm. This value is formed by the limited energy at these wavelength levels to generate the cytotoxic molecule. The selection of an appropriate light source is based on the capability of PS to absorb at this wavelength, the size of tumors, the location and accessibility of the disease, as well as on the costs [4,5].

Oxygen constitutes an important factor for this therapy. Its presence is activated with the triplet state of PS and, via determined energy pathways, ROS are produced. These species are responsible for the damage of tumor cells. During irradiation, oxygen is depleted and this can be measured either by the hypoxic cell fraction in the tumor immediately after PDT or by directly measuring tissue oxygen tension during irradiation with the aid of oxygen electrodes [5].

Another considerable parameter for the therapy is the time interval between the administration of the drug and the irradiation. Namely, if the time between these two stages is long, PS has the potential to diffuse from normal tissues and consequently it accumulates in tumor cells. In this case, phototoxic side effects are minimized. However, irradiating shortly after PS administration will cause more efficient vascular damage due to the existence of photosensitizer drug in blood vessels [4].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon & graphene quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para:
Several methods have been developed to synthesize carbon-based quantum dots. The synthesis method is crucial for the material's properties and determines its potential to be used in PDT. Synthesis methods can be divided into two main approaches: a) Top-down approaches and b) Bottom-up approaches. In the first route, bulk materials are broken down with the aid of energy, such as thermal, chemical, and sonication. Bottom-up approaches use molecular precursors to produce carbon-based quantum dots [8,10,11,28–30]. The general synthesis approaches and the main methods to fabricate carbon-based quantum dots are presented in Figs. 9 and 10
respectively.

Generally, top-down methods require readily available bulk starting materials such as graphene oxide, multi-wall carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs), etc., and some of the necessary reagents for the synthesis are harsh and corrosive. However, recently, some alternative environmentally friendly reagents have been presented in the literature. Top-down methods are considered to be more complex synthesis routes than bottom-up strategies. Another limitation is the difficulty of heteroatom doping of the carbon dots’ surface [11,13].

On the other hand, bottom-up synthesis routes rely on small molecules as starting materials, and the formation of carbon dots is mainly achieved through high temperature and pressure conditions. A major advantage of these methods is the convenience of adding heteroatoms to the carbon dots’ surface. However, in the case of hydrothermal synthesis, surface passivation is considered a time-consuming process. The microwave route solves this issue and also secures a uniform size of carbon dots due to simultaneous heating. A concern regarding this category of synthesis is the purification of carbon dots from precursors and by-products. The physicochemical properties of carbon-based quantum dots are regulated by the suitable precursor molecules, and bottom-up synthesis is considered to be more efficient in terms of the yield of synthesized carbon dots [13,31]. In Table 1 , the advantages and disadvantages of each synthetic route are highlighted.

In order to select the appropriate synthetic route, some important factors should be taken into consideration, such as aggregation during carbonization, the control of particle size and homogeneity, and the passivation of carbon dots’ surface. Passivation is a highly important factor since it strongly affects solubility and photoluminescence, which are crucial in PDT [28]. Additionally, starting materials and specific conditions of each synthesis route can determine the final option of a synthesis method, as these factors influence the final properties of the synthesized materials [13]. To justify this issue, we present a list of synthesized CQDs and GrQDs with their precursors and physical properties categorized according to their synthetic approach (Tables 2 and 3
).

Chemical oxidation constitutes an ideal method to produce CQDs and GrQDs on a large scale and requires application of oxidizing acids. Carbon soot, coal, carbon black, graphite, graphene, graphene oxide, carbon nanotubes, carbon fibers, and activated carbon are commonly used as carbon sources. Another important fact is that the negatively charged oxygenated carbonyl-, carboxyl-, and hydroxyl-groups, can be introduced in carbon dots, leading to an increase of their hydrophilicity making them ideal candidates in bio-related applications, such as PDT and drug delivery. The disadvantages of this method include the need for harsh conditions, the inconvenience of removing excess acid, and the lack of control over carbon dot sizes [10,13,28,29]. Regarding the development of non-uniform carbon dots, it is explained by the fact that bulk precursors are being cut off at the weak intercalation sites [13]. Formation of doped carbon dots with heteroatoms is considered difficult through this strategy, and surface defects are also generated during the reaction [13,29].

An example of a chemical oxidation approach is the synthesis of CQDs from carbohydrates with the addition of acids. To be more specific, concentrated sulfuric acid dehydrates raw material and then nitric acid is added in order to obtain nanoparticles. Enhanced photoluminescence of CQDs is achieved with the use of amine-terminated compounds [33]. In 2019, Gunjal et al. [34] presented the synthesis of carbon dots through chemical oxidation for tetracycline detection in urine and pharmaceutical samples and for yeast cell imaging. Nitric acid was the oxidative agent, and waste tea residue was the carbon source, which is easily accessible and capable of producing luminescent carbon dots (see Fig. 11 a). The use of strong oxidative agents and the subsequent generation of chemical waste in this strategy can cause harm to the environment. However, in some cases, it can be used environmentally friendly acids such as hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) [13,81]. In 2019, Feng and Zhang [35] presented a green way to synthesize CQDs for light-emitting diodes (LEDs) via chemical oxidation. The CQDs were produced from coke powder and H2O2, without using mixtures of strong acids. J. Wen et al. [36] suggested the combination of two mild oxidizing agents (H2O2 and O3) for GrQDs’ synthesis (see Fig. 11b). The carbon dots had graphene oxide as a carbon precursor and were capable to detect electrochemically ions due to their abundance in –COOH groups.

Coal is often referred as an optimum precursor for chemical oxidation due to its structure and affordability compared to other carbon sources [13,82]. Zhang et al. [37] fabricated GrQDs based on coal via chemical oxidation (see Fig. 12 ). Especially, bituminous coal was used as bulk precursor while H2SO4 and HNO3 were the oxidizing agents. The as-synthesized GrQDs were quite dispersible with sizes ranging from 2.0 to 3.2 nm having oxygen-contained groups such as –COOH, –CO and C–O–C. Liu et al. [38] have used lignite coal as starting material to synthesize CQDs for the detection of Fe3+. Ozone was used as an acid in order to substitute harsh chemicals, and the derived CQDs had abundant oxygen functional groups and the optical properties required for ion detection.

Chemical oxidation can be combined with hydrothermal/solvothermal or microwave strategies in order to improve the oxidation with the help of enhanced temperature. Shin et al. [39] developed GrQDs from bulk precursors such as graphite, MWCNTs, carbon fiber, and charcoal, combining chemical oxidation with a solvothermal process. Principally, the starting carbonaceous materials were cut off with the help of oxone (potassium monopersulfate), which was the oxidizing agent, and the solvent dimethylformamide (DMF) in an autoclave reactor. In 2016, Luo et al. [40] combined chemical oxidation with a microwave route to synthesize GrQDs for enhanced white LEDs (see Fig. 13 ). Graphite was used as carbon source while a mixture of H2SO4 and HNO3 was applied as an oxidizing agent. Afterwards, the synthesized GQrDs were further treated through microwave irradiation in an alkaline environment.

Electrochemical oxidation is a widely used technique for producing CQDs and GrQDs. The main advantages are the enhanced purity of products, the improved yield, the easily control of particles' size, as well as the reproducibility [10,28,29,41]. Electrodes and electrolyte are the two crucial parameters of carbon dots’ properties, such as photoluminescence and surface states. Common carbon sources as electrodes are carbon nanotubes, graphite rods, and platinum sheets, which are in an electrolyte solution. Some common electrolytes are ionic liquids and sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4). One environmentally friendly and cheap way to synthesize CQDs is the use of alcohols in an alkaline environment. In this synthetic route, a graphite electrode was used, and CQDs change color when exposed at room temperature [41]. Recently, Kalita et al. [42] presented the electrochemical synthesis of GrQDs using graphene oxide and LiClO4 as electrode and electrolyte, respectively, in propylene carbonate (see Fig. 14 ). The size of GrQDs can be controlled by the concentration of electrolyte, the applied potential, and the oxidation time. The produced GrQDs were investigated as potential soil moisture sensors.

Commonly used carbon sources are considered to be expensive, and thus recent studies present alternative options for the synthesis of carbon dots based on electrochemical oxidation [13]. Kumar et al. [43] fabricated GrQDs electrochemically from waste dry cell batteries (see Fig. 15 ). Namely, graphite rod was removed from dry batteries, which constitute the electrodes and citric acid and NaOH were the electrolytes. This method is evaluated as affordable, and the synthesized GrQDs are capable of being used as biosensors. Graphite rods were also utilized to synthesize GrQDs with a mixture of citric acid and NaOH as the electrolyte. The oxygen functional groups of GrQDs can be increased by varying concentrations of NaOH. Additionally, graphite rods are heated at high temperatures before the synthesis, and this technique creates more surface defects and consequently more available oxidation sites [44].

Electrochemical oxidation is an attractive way to synthesize carbon dots due to its convenience in doping. Nitrogen-doped GrQDs (N-GrQDs) were fabricated electrochemically by using graphite rods as electrode and the binary mixture of NaOH and semicarbazide as electrolyte. The synthesized GrQDs were abundant in nitrogen and oxygen functional groups and can be used as detectors for Fe3+ [45]. Zhou et al. [46] presented the electrochemical synthesis of nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) using a pre-baked carbon anode and ammonium bicarbonate as the electrolyte. The main synthesis parameters were the starting materials, the control of electrochemical oxidation and the following thermal decomposition for CQDs purification. This synthetic route allows the large-scale production of CQDs, which were investigated as potential inhibitors of copper corrosion. Recently, iron-nitrogen doped CQDs (Fe,N-CQDs) were synthesized via an electrochemical route for the detection of Cu2+ ions. The electrode consists of carbon cloth that was covered with monoatomic iron-anchored nitrogen-doped carbon (Fe–N–C). The Fe–N–C is prepared by MgO, FeCl3·6H2O, and 1,10-phenanthroline, and then it is mixed with polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE). A combination of NaOH, C2H5OH, and H2O was the electrolyte for doped CQDs’ synthesis. Various PTFE concentration and voltage conditions were applied in order to obtain different samples of Fe,N-CQDs [47].

Laser ablation constitutes a synthetic route in which solid material is irradiated with a laser beam in a solution [32]. The main features of this method are simplicity, flexibility, and the ability to control carbon dots' size [10,29,32]. However, laser ablation is characterized by low quantum yields (QY) and the necessity of surface modification [32]. One example of laser ablation is the synthesis of CQDs by graphite powder in a polyethylene glycol (PEG) solution for several values of laser pulse width. This parameter affects interaction time between graphite and irradiation, and it is responsible for the nucleation and growth of CQDs. Other parameters for controlling CQDs’ size are for instance time irradiation and laser fluence [32]. Recently, Li et al. [48] developed CQDs with the laser ablation route for modification of perovskite solar cell (see Fig. 16 a). Carbon nanomaterials were the precursors of CQDs while and the size has been controlled by the laser fluence. A novel technique for production of GrQDs via the laser route was suggested by Zhang et al. [49], which used a laser writing instrument and graphite paper to form GrQDs (see Fig. 16b). The synthesized materials had uniform size, emitted blue fluorescence, and the yield can be improved by regulating the laser scan speed and the output power. This method is simple, efficient, does not require the use of harsh chemicals, and can produce GrQDs on a large scale. Shen et al. [50] fabricated N-GrQDs via laser ablation for the detection of Fe3+. Graphene and ammonia were the carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively, and the as-synthesized GrQDs exhibited high photoluminescence. This study suggests an inexpensive and effective way to synthesize N-GrQDs via laser ablation, compared to previous ones that utilize unaffordable precursors.

This top-down approach is used to break down bulk materials with alternate high and low pressure ultrasound waves in liquid creating intense hydrodynamic shear forces [10,12,29]. The main parameters in this approach are the ratio of carbon precursors, solvents, reaction time, and ultrasonic power. Ultrasonic treatment constitutes a simple way to produce CQDs and GrQDs, although the high energy cost appears to be the main disadvantage [10,12]. This kind of synthesis utilizes a variety of carbon materials, such as graphene oxide, carbon fibers, carbon nanotubes, and graphite [83]. Routh et al. [51] fabricated GrQDs from graphene oxide via a sono-Fenton reaction approach in order to be used as a component in the construction of a dye-sensitized solar cell. Graphene oxide was dissolved in deionized water and, after the addition of H2O2 and FeCl3, the mixture was sonicated to produce GrQDs.

Ultrasonic treatment is also used as a synthetic path for carbon-based quantum dots production in combination with the hydrothermal method. Park et al. [52] synthesized CQDs by means of ultrasonic synthesis using food waste as a raw material. In this environmentally friendly way, ultrasound contributes to dehydration, polymerization, and carbonization, leading to the nucleation of CQDs. Afterwards, diffusion has a major role in growth without the need for surface modification. The recent study published by Qi et al. [53] presented the synthesis of N-CQDs via ultrasonic treatment by using l-glutamic acid as the carbon source material. The synthesized CQDs exhibited superior optical properties and were applied for BV2 cell imaging. Wang et al. [54] used one more combinational ultrasonic-hydrothermal strategy to synthesize N-GrQDs (see Fig. 17 ). The combination of these two routes offered mild reaction conditions, reduced reaction time and energy consumption, and protected the synthesized material from agglomeration. Citric acid and L-glutamic were used as starting materials, and the produced GrQDs were highly fluorescent with excellent photostability.

Microwave synthesis is a common procedure to prepare carbon dots due to the short reaction time, low cost, and uniformity of nanomaterials that can be achieved through simultaneous heating [10,13,28,29,84]. All these advantages constitute the microwave route as a competitive way to synthesize CQDs and GrQDs on a large scale [13]. Microwaves belong to the portion of electromagnetic spectrum with wavelength in the range between 1 mm and 1 m, which refers to energy capable of breaking off chemical bonds of precursor compounds [10,28,29]. However, the microwave route is not recommended for synthesis of carbon dots in applications that require a larger size of the particles. Furthermore, there is a limit on the maximum size of the reaction samples due to the limited penetration of microwave irradiation into substrates. It is suggested to evaluate the crystallinity of the synthesized materials in the case of microwave synthesis of GrQDs. Crystallinity can be improved with the adjustment of microwave heating time and power [13].

The first microwave synthesis of CQDs utilizes glucose and PEG. The CQDs’ formation is confirmed by the gradual color change of the solution from colorless to yellow and finally to brown [55]. A green way to synthesize CQDs via the microwave method is by using orange peels waste as precursors (see Fig. 18 a). In this study, CQDs showed high photoluminescence, great chemical stability, and abundance in carboxylic and amine groups from the precursor and thus they were further successfully used as photosensitizers for TiO2 [56]. In another recent study, microwave irradiation was used to synthesize CQDs, using Beta vulgaris (beetroot) as a carbon source. The produced CQDs were conjugated with aloe vera to heal chronic wounds [57]. Microwave irradiation can be used to form GrQDs, which can act as detectors for Al3+ ions. In this research work, glucose was used as carbon source material while ethylene glycol was the solvent. The synthesis was based on the ring-closure condensation of the precursor molecules. The synthesized GrQDs exhibit high sensitivity and selectivity towards investigated ions [58]. In another recent study, the microwave synthesis of GrQDs by biomass waste was presented (see Fig. 18b). Namely, in that work spent tea waste was oxidized by HNO3 via microwave heating. Microwave power and time of heating were the main parameters for the synthesis. The synthesized GrQDs showed great sensitivity towards Fe3+ ions [59].

Microwave treatment is among the synthesizing methods that facilitate heteroatom doping of CQDs and GrQDs [13]. Recently, N-CQDs were prepared via the microwave strategy with the aid of a deep eutectic solvent (DES). Especially, choline chloride-urea (ChCl) is the non-toxic used DES that constitutes the required nitrogen doping agent and the solvent for the synthesis. Citric acid was the carbon source, and the produced N-CQDs were applied in RAW264.7 cell imaging [60]. In 2022, researchers fabricated boron-nitrogen doped CQDs (B,N-CQDs) for the detection of picric acid, a common pollutant in industries (see Fig. 19 a). Firstly, N-CQDs were prepared by l-serine and citric acid via the microwave method. Boric acid is the boron dopant that was added in the next step, and the final mixture was kept under reflux condition [61]. N-GrQDs were fabricated via microwave synthesis using waste precursors (see Fig. 19b). In this study, melamine sponge and arjuna bark were the nitrogen and carbon source materials, respectively. The produced GrQDs were applied to MDA-MB-231 cell imaging and H2O2 detection [62]. In another study, fluorine-doped GrQDs (F-GrQDs) were synthesized by means of microwave irradiation to prevent the aggregation of peptides such as hIAPP. For the synthesis, glucose was used as carbon source and hydrofluoric acid (HF) was the fluorine dopant. The choice of fluorine as a doping agent lies in the ability to improve the metabolic activity of the organic material and to promote the α-helix structure in proteins in order to prevent the aggregation [63].

In hydrothermal or solvothermal treatment, organic raw material is treated in an autoclave reactor at high pressure and temperature conditions. This route is economically, environmentally friendly and many precursors, such as saccharides, organic acids, and food waste can be utilized [10,29]. This synthesis approach is ideal to synthesize CQDs and GrQDs for biomedical applications due to its ability to produce small amounts of material with a high photoluminescence quantum yield. However, this method is not a rapid synthesis route, it requires intense purification, and the produced carbon dots may have small particle sizes and face aggregation [13]. A recent example of a solvothermal method is the synthesis of CQDs for inkjet printing (see Fig. 20 ). The CQDs were fabricated using l-ascorbic acid as carbon source material and deionized water and ethanol as solvents. The fabricated CQDs were dissolved in solvents with various polarities, and parameters, such as contact angle and viscosity, were investigated in order to optimize the inks for inkjet printing on bond paper [64]. Lei et al. [65] presented the hydrothermal synthesis of N-GrQDs by citric acid and urea. The synthesized N-GrQDs were further treated solvothermally with SnO2 nanoparticles and HCl in order to investigate the photoelectric properties of the prepared nanocomposites.

A large number of studies have been reported in the literature for the application of environmentally friendly precursors in carbon dot synthesis in order to facilitate large scale production with an affordable approach and to avoid the use of harsh chemicals. An example of green hydrothermal treatment is the use of sugar cane juice as a carbon source (see Fig. 21 a). In particular, sugar cane juice and ethanol are sealed in an autoclave reactor for 180 min at 120 °C and the colorless solution turns brown [66]. Red lentils were used as a precursor in the hydrothermal synthesis of CQDs for Fe3+ detection (see Fig. 21b). The synthesized sensor is affordable, easily synthesized with selectivity and sensitivity towards the desired ions, making this method of synthesis ideal for large scale production [67]. Yu et al. [68] presented osmanthus fragrans as a precursor for hydrothermal synthesis of CQDs (see Fig. 21c). Osmanthus fragrans is abundant in nitrogen and therefore it can act as a nitrogen dopant for CQDs. The synthesized N-CQDs exhibit blue fluorescence, high quantum yield and were applied in quercetin and Al3+ detection as well as in imaging of the T24 cell line. Khose et al. [69] have used guava leaf as an alternative green precursor to synthesize red fluorescent GrQDs. The GrQDs were produced via the hydrothermal method and were used for sensing Hg2+ ion.

The main advantage of hydrothermal treatment is the ability to passivate and introduce heteroatoms on the carbon dots’ surface. Various studies of doped CQDs and GrQDs have been reported recently in the literature. For instance, Qu et al. [70] fabricated nitrogen-sulfur doped CQDs (N,S-CQDs) using l-cysteine as carbon source as well as nitrogen and sulfur dopant material. A mixture of HNO3 and ethylene glycol were the solvents, which were transferred with the l-cysteine in an autoclave reactor. The synthesized CQDs were combined with TiO2 and Bi2MoO6 to produce a film with photocatalytic and antimicrobial properties. In addition, Shi et al. [71] reported recently the hydrothermal synthesis of N,S-CQDs (see Fig. 22 a). In this study, l-cysteine and polyethyleneimine were the nitrogen and sulfur doping agents, and the fabricated CQDs were applied for sensing of Pb2+. Furthermore, Wang et al. [72], have used hydrothermally synthesized N-CQDs for the detection of ClO− and Cr(VI). The precursors for this synthesis were the chelating agent Methyl Glycine Diacetic acid Trisodium Salt (MGDA) and ethylenediamine. It is also interesting that Li et al. [73] developed multimodal imaging nanoprobe based on CQDs via hydrothermal synthesis. Specifically, CQDs were doped with manganese and dysprosium with starting materials such as aminophylline hydrate, Na2EDTA, citric acid, MnCl2·4H2O and DyCl3·6H2O. In addition, Ren et al. [74] fabricated N-CQDs solvothermally using natural deep eutectic solvents (NADESs) for the detection of Co2+ under high saline conditions (see Fig. 22b). In this study, the used NADESs were based on 1H-Benzotriazole (BTA) and chlorine chloride, and anhydrous ethanol was the solvent. Finally, Juang et al. [75] synthesized boron doped GrQDs (B-GrQDs) by means of solvothermal method (see Fig. 22c). The carbon source was 1,3,6-trinitropyrene (TNP), the boron dopant was boric acid, while the solvent was DMF. The synthesized GrQDs exhibited green and red fluorescence, and the photoluminescence intensities of the GrQDs can be enhanced by varying the boric acid concentration and the chemical ratio of boric acid to TNP.

In pyrolysis, an organic chemical compound is dehydrated and carbonized at extremely high temperatures to produce carbon dots. Some advantages of this thermal decomposition method are as follows: production of large-scale carbon dots, low cost, as well as rapid and easily synthesis process for CQDs and GrQDs [10,29]. However, it must be mentioned that the produced carbon dots are not uniform in size [10].

Cheng et al. [76] have used a pyrolysis method combined with chemical cutting in order to produce CQDs from walnut (see Fig. 23a). In particular, walnut shells were carbonized at 1000 °C and the produced nanomaterials were used for cellular imaging. One more way to produce CQDs via this synthetic route is to use pork rib bones as a precursor. The bones were carbonized in a furnace at 700 °C, and an acid oxidation treatment with H2SO4 and HNO3 followed. By using pork rib bones as a precursor, the resultant materials were doped with calcium, sulfur, and nitrogen. CQDs were used to detect the organophosphorus pesticide, dimethoate [77]. Ganesan et al. [78] presented a green synthesis process of CQDs through a pyrolysis technique with the aid of microwave irradiation (see Fig. 23b). Specifically, Ziziphus mauritiania stone was used as a carbon precursor, which was carbonized in a furnace under N2 atmosphere. Afterwards, the material was further treated in a microwave oven. The Ziziphus Mauritania stone is selected due to its high carbon content and its availability. The produced CQDs were applied for the detection of ammonia in environmental samples. The pyrolysis method was also used to produce GrQDs from biopolymers. In particular, alginic sodium salt beads from brown algae were pyrolized in an argon atmosphere up to 900 °C [79]. Kir et al. [80] recently fabricated GrQDs using molasses as a carbon source material, which is a byproduct of sugar beet processing. The carbon source was carbonized in an ash furnace at various temperatures in order to optimize the conditions of pyrolysis. The elemental and optical properties of fabricated GrQDs were also investigated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Essential characteristics of carbon-based quantum dots to photodynamic therapy

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon-based quantum dots exhibit the highest intensities in the UV region, and it can be observed declination towards the red spectral region (Figs. 24, 26 and 27 ). For the carbon dots’ absorbance π-π* transitions of CC bonds and n-π* transitions of CO bonds are responsible [16,85]. The transitions of CC bonds enable carbon-based quantum dots to be effective in photon-harvesting, which is evident in the appearance of maximum absorbance in the short-wavelength region [29,86]. The CQDs are considered to absorb at longer wavelengths compared to GrQDs [29]. However, the tendency of carbon dots to absorb in the blue region leads to hindrance in application of PDT to deeper tissue. This difficulty can be resolved with the conjugation of carbon dots with molecules such as phthalocyanines that absorb in the red region. Such as complexes can effectively treat deeper lesions and utilize the strong photoluminescent properties of carbon-based quantum dots by securing sufficient energy and increasing singlet oxygen production [87]. The absorbance of carbon dots can be affected by the synthesis method and precursors as well as by surface functional groups [16]. Yang et al. [88] fabricated solvothermally blue, green, and red CQDs by using various solvents such as ethyl acetate, ethanol, acetone, formamide, and water. This study demonstrated that solvent is able to tune the optical properties of CQDs and the red shift is attributed to the increase in CO groups, conjugated sp2 domains, and a decrease in pyridinic nitrogen. The study of Tang et al. [89] showed the role of functional groups in CQDs absorbance. In particular, researchers synthesized pristine CQDs (B-CQDs), nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-CQDs), sulfur-doped CQDs (S-CQDs) and phosphorus-doped CQDs (P-CQDs). In the case of pristine CQDs, there is an abundance of –COOH functional groups, of which a part of them is converted into –CO and other functional groups including NH2,-SO2, –HSO3, and H2PO4 after doping modification. Doped-CQDs exhibited two separate absorption peaks at 253 nm and 303 nm as a result of the π-π* transition of the CC bond and n-π* transition of the CO bond, respectively. These absorption bands show the existence of sp2-conjugated domains in the core of doped CQDs and oxygen-containing groups (CO, O–CO/COOH) on their surface. On the other hand, pristine CQDs exhibit only one absorption peak at 278 nm.

Some researchers make use of the influence of precursor compounds in the absorption spectrum of carbon-based quantum dots in order to shift the nanomaterials' absorbance in the visible region of the spectrum. For instance, Jiang et al. [90] recently synthesized CQDs based on zinc phthalocyanine and citric acid. In this case, the absorption of zinc phthalocyanine at longer wavelengths was inherited by the synthesized carbon nanomaterials, which can absorb at 670 nm (see Fig. 25
) and were applied as photodynamic and photothermal agents. In another study, quinoline derivatives were the parent carbon sources used to synthesize CQDs, and this resulted in CQDs’ absorption peak at around 630 nm. In addition to this, quinoline derivatives offered nucleic acid targeting [91]. In 2019, Liu et al. [92] developed CQDs rich in chlorophyll using spinach leaves. The synthesized carbon nanomaterials exhibited a peak at 660 nm, and this facilitated their use as tumor diagnosis and treatment PDT medicine. Deep-tissue penetration was also achieved with CQDs synthesized by glutathione. In this case, CQDs showed absorbance peaks at 265 nm, 400 nm, 610 nm, and 680 nm [93].

Photoluminescence (PL) of carbon-based quantum dots refers to their ability to emit photons as a result of photon absorbance and the subsequent excitation of electrons at higher energy level. Photon emission is attributed to the return of electrons to a stable energy state. Carbon-Based Quantum Dots show strong PL in solution and solid state in the visible to near infrared. This means that carbon dots exhibit different color emissions with the variation of wavelength [13,94]. Non-blinking PL and superb photostability characterize carbon-based quantum dots compared to organic or inorganic fluorophores, since such features offer tracking of a molecule and long-term real time imaging, respectively [27,85]. PL is of great importance for PDT, and the reason for this observation is still under examination [13,95]. However, this review summarizes the three possible mechanisms of PL based on reports published in literature.

The surface state of carbon dots is the most accepted mechanism, and it is based on the degree of surface oxidation and surface functional groups such as CO and CN. Regarding surface oxidation, an increase in oxygen content is associated with a red shift in carbon-based quantum dots’ emission. Namely, surface oxidation results to the enhancement of defect sites, which collect excitons, and the generated radiation leads to red-shift emission [96]. The importance of surface oxidation as a PL mechanism was declared by Ding et al. [97], which fabricated hydrothermally carbon dots by urea and p-phenylenediamine. Carbon dots were separated via silica column chromatography and the samples exhibited different PL characteristics. Structural characterization proved the similarity in particles size. Furthermore, it has been observed that highly-oxidized samples had emission peaks shifted into the red region of the spectrum. Surface functional groups affect the emission centers of carbon dots and consequently, they can regulate photoluminescence. In case of similar oxygen content, the surface functional group is the major PL factor compared to the degree of surface oxidation [96]. An example of such PL mechanism has been presented by Zhang et al. [98], who synthesized solvothermally seven multicolor samples of carbon dots varying the ratio of used precursors (ammonium citrate and ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid) in DMF. In this study, the difference in emission peaks of the carbon dots was mainly due to the changes in content of surface functional groups CO and CN, as the samples had similar oxygen content according to XPS analysis.

The quantum confinement effect constitutes another possible explanation for carbon dots' PL, while the dimensions and the aromatic rings in the carbon dots' structure constitute vital factors [96]. This phenomenon was observed in semiconductors with sizes less than the Bohr radius and refers to the spatial confinement of excitons in three dimensions [99]. In the study by Kim et al. [100], the quantum confinement effect is presented as a major factor for red shift emission in GrQDs' peaks when their size increases. Particularly, GrQDs with sizes of 5–35 nm exhibited a red shift in absorption and emission peaks as the GrQDs’ size increases. However, the absorption energy tends to decrease with the increase of diameter of GrQDs.

One last luminescence mechanism is molecular fluorescence, in which carbon dots’ emission is attributed to fluorescent impurities generated from bottom-up synthesis [96]. Such mechanism was presented in the study of Song et al. [101], in which the fluorescent molecule imidazo[1,2-a]pyridine-7-carboxylic acid, 1,2,3,5-tetrahydro-5-oxo-(IPCA) was identified in carbon dots. In this research work, carbon dots with high quantum yield were synthesized hydrothermally by citric acid and ethylenediamine. It was found that the synthesized carbon dots after purification consisted of IPCA, polymers, and carbon cores. The strong fluorescence was attributed to IPCA and its derivatives. The other components contribute to the carbon core state PL, while IPCA to the molecular state one.

Each mechanism contributes differently to the total PL effect of CQDs and GrQDs. Quantum dots based on graphene are considered to be more photoluminescent materials compared to CQDs, as GrQDs exhibit higher Quantum Yield (QY). The layered structure and high level of crystallinity are responsible for the strong PL in GrQDs. The PL properties of GrQDs can be attributed to π electrons in sp2 clusters of crystal lattice, zigzag/armchair edge states, heteroatom doping, shape, functional groups, and defects [29,102]. Sk et al. [103] demonstrated by employing density functional theory (DFT) and time-dependent density functional theory (TDDFT) that all these factors mentioned above can tune GrQDs' PL. Especially, fluorescence emission varies from UV (235.2 nm) to near infrared (999.5 nm) with GrQDs' size from 0.46 to 2.31 nm. An increase in GrQDs' size is reflected by a red-shift in emission peaks, mainly due to π-electron delocalization and a following reduction in band gap. Vacancy defects affect strongly the PL of GrQDs, since they cause a red shift in fluorescence. Regarding edge states of GrQDs, zigzag-edged GrQDs with localized sites mainly in the edge sites decrease the band gap and consequently cause red-shift in emission, while armchair edged GrQDs with localized states scattered in the center show blue shift emission. The attachment of –OH groups to the basal plane of GrQDs caused a red-shift in the emission peaks of GrQDs. Similar results are obtained with the conjugation of other oxygen-containing groups such as –COOH to the edge plane of GrQDs. In contrast to oxygen-containing groups, pyrrolic nitrogen atoms attached to the edges of GrQDs are responsible for the blue shift in the fluorescence emission. The addition of nitrogen into the interior of GrQDs exhibits opposite results compared with those of pyrrolic nitrogen. A blue shift can be observed in sulfur-doped GrQDs, while a red shift can be seen in boron-doped GrQDs. Regarding all the above, heteroatoms are crucial to GrQDs’ PL along with other factors such as concentration of doping agent and final structure of GrQDs [13].

On the other hand, CQDs show less PL than GrQDs due to emissive traps on their surface. The quantum confinement effect and sp2 clusters of carbon network do not dominate as mechanisms in CQDs PL. Radiative recombination of surface trapped electrons and holes are considered to give PL properties in CQDs. Electron acceptors and donors have the ability to disrupt radiative recombination, resulting in the observed quenching of CQDs' PL. It is possible to enhance CQDs' PL by surface passivation with heteroatoms, since the CQDs’ surface becomes more stable and can enable radiative recombination [29,102,104,105].

Carbon-based quantum dots' PL dependents also on pH, since surface functional groups can undergo deprotonation and protonation by varying pH values [13,31]. In study reported by Guo et al. [106], GrQDs were synthesized with bifunctional groups –OH and –NH2, and the pH dependence of GrQDs’ PL was investigated. The results demonstrated that amine-functionalized GrQDs changed fluorescence colors from blue to yellow in the pH range from 5.0 to 10.0. This behavior can be attributed to protonation or deprotonation of –NH2 groups in an acidic or alkaline environment, respectively. In contrast to amine-functionalized GrQDs, GrQDs with –OH groups do not exhibit any pH-Dependence.

Biocompatibility is a crucial parameter for the successful treatment of cancer with PDT, because a biocompatible material is compatible with cells or living tissue without causing any toxicity or being carcinogenic [108]. The biocompatibility of any proposed PS must be carefully examined in order to avoid toxicity and severe health damage. Generally, biocompatibility can be assessed via in vitro and in vivo biocompatibility studies. In the first case, the toxicity of the PS is tested on various cell lines, which is a simple technique compared to in vivo studies. Mice, zebrafish, and nematodes are some common testers for in vivo biocompatibility research [109].

Carbon-based quantum dots are considered to be biocompatible materials compared to other metal-based nanoparticles due to their superior water solubility and lack of metal residues [14]. A large number of studies have been focused on investigation of biocompatibility of CQDs in various cell lines by measuring their cytotoxicity [108]. For instance, in the recent study reported by Tian et al. [110], the synthesized CQDs were evaluated regarding their cytotoxicity in cell lines NP69, CNE2, HepG2 and RAW264.7. In vitro studies were conducted at different concentrations of CQDs incubation time and CQDs proved to have excellent biocompatibility. Similar results were obtained when the cytotoxicity of CQDs was tested on cell lines HT1080 and WRL68. To be more specific, no morphological changes were observed in the examined cell lines when they were treated with various concentrations of CQDs [111]. Zebrafish Danio rerio is a commonly used animal for in vivo biocompatibility studies because it presents fast embryonic development, high transparency, and similarities with mammals [112]. Gadolinium-sulfur-nitrogen-doped CQDs were tested in zebrafish embryos alone and conjugated with folic acid. Early-stage embryos were incubated with various concentrations of GdNS@CQDs and FA-GdNS@CQDs, and the results were presented in Fig. 28 . Zebrafish embryos exhibited normal survival and hatching rates at concentrations up to 100 μg/ml, while no significant abnormalities were reported at concentrations up to 1000 μg/ml [113].

Regarding the biocompatibilty of GrQDs, they are considered non-toxic and have been recently tested in various cell lines. In fact, GrQDs with small lateral sizes are ideal for biorelated applications since are unable to damage the membranes of cells [13]. Even at high GrQDs concentrations, a breast cancer cell line (MDA-MB-231) showed 70% cell viability [62]. In a recent study, the biocompatiblity of N-GrQDs was also evaluated in other breast cancer cell lines such as MCF-7 and SK-BR-3, and the cells survived after incubation with various concentrations of GrQDs [114]. HeLa cell viability of above 80% was mentioned when the cells were incubated with different concentrations of GrQDs based on the polysaccharide konjac glucomannan [115]. GrQDs were also found to be biocompatible with melanoma SK-Mel-2 cells up to a concentration of 200 μg/ml [116]. The biocompatibility of GrQDs has also been tested recently in embryonic neural stem progenitor cells. In this case, GrQDs were functionalized with polyethylene glycol (PEG) and showed no any toxicity up to a concentration of 320 μg/ml [117]. Chong et al. [118] studied the in vivo biocompatibility of PEG-functionalized GrQDs in Balb/c mice. The results showed that GrQDs-PEG accumulated mainly in the kidney, compared with other organs such as the liver, lung, heart, etc. (see Fig. 29 ). EPR effect contributed to the accumulation of GrQDs-PEG at the tumor site. Fast renal clearance and low distribution in major organs make the examined nanomaterial biocompatible. Functionalized graphene oxide with PEG (GO-PEG) was also tested for comparison reasons, and evident changes in the spleen and liver of mice were reported in this case.

A useful characteristic of carbon dots is their ability to act as donors and acceptors in the Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) phenomenon. FRET describes the energy transfer without radiation between two fluorophores in order to regulate the desired properties of a material. FRET fluorophores are characterized by sensitivity and selectivity [119]. Carbon-based quantum dots have an extensive range of excitation and emission wavelengths, and this allows their contribution to FRET as energy donors or acceptors. This feature is important for PDT efficiency since carbon dots mainly act as energy donors in known PSs and secure the production of ROS [120].

The production of ROS and singlet oxygen is vital for PDT efficiency. Carbon-based quantum dots are used as PSs in the PDT of cancer, in which free radicals are produce via two photochemical pathways. Type I photochemistry describes the excited electron transfer to oxygen in order to produce OH− and
O 2 −
. Carbon dots follow this path as a result of charge separation, in which excited electrons move to the conduction band (CB), leaving holes in the valence band (VB). In the CB, excited electrons react with the oxygen molecule to produce free radicals, and the holes in the VB generate OH− due to interactions with water molecules. Type II photochemistry is an energy transfer process to oxygen with the aim of producing singlet oxygen. Carbon-based quantum dots form a pair of an excited electron at the minimum CB and a hole at the maximum VB, which is called exciton. The formation of exciton leads to the release of energy, and intersystem crossing occurs, in which excited singlet exciton moves to a triplet state. The released energy is transferred to ground molecular oxygen in order to produce singlet oxygen. Both Type I and Type II photochemistry pathways are essential properties of carbon-based quantum dots for PDT application [121].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recent applications of carbon-based quantum dots in photodynamic therapy
ce:section.ce:para: Recently, researchers utilize CQDs and GrQDs in PDT in order to treat various types of cancer, such as brain, lung, breast, and skin cancer. Water solubility, absorbance in longer wavelengths, no toxicity, and fluorescence are the main desirable features of carbon dots, that make them potential tools for cancer therapy (treatment) and diagnosis. Carbon-based quantum dots can be applied in PDT as PSs itself or in conjugation with other molecules. In some studies, the efficiency of carbon dots as PSs alone with nitrogen, sulfur, or phosphorus as dopants was investigated, while in other studies the interest was focused on the precursors of carbon dots. To explain this more, some researchers fabricated CQDs with known PSs such as manganese phthalocyanine or curcumin as precursors. Another possible option is the conjugation of carbon dots with known PSs. Some common PSs for conjugation are chlorin e6 (Ce6), ZnTPPcQ, PtPor, and PPIX. In addition, PSs based on carbon-based quantum dots were further linked with targeting molecules in order to enhance their selectivity to tumor tissues. One example is the use of folic acid, which is overexpressed in cancer cells. The efficiency of the PS in treatment requires sufficient knowledge of the tumor microenvironment. For instance, tumors are characterized by hypoxia and the microenvironment is abundant with hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). Some studies deploy these characteristics and are focused on the synthesis of PSs with metal ions such as copper or manganese ions that react with H2O2 to produce ROS and oxygen. Some other applications of CQDs and GrQDs to PDT refer to the development of drug delivery systems with the purpose of delivering successfully to tumor sites drugs such as doxorubicin (DOX) and methotrexate (MTX). This section highlights the most recent synthesized PSs for PDT based on carbon-based quantum dots (Table 4 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon-based quantum dots in treatment of breast cancer with PDT
ce:section.ce:para: Breast cancer is a common cancer type in women, and PDT is being investigated as a less invasive treatment due to the accessibility of the breast area to light. One of the main advantages of the use of PDT for breast cancer is the restoration of skin and normal tissue without scars by protecting collagen and elastin during therapy [167]. Various experiments based on the human breast cancer cell line MCF-7 have been recently conducted. Xu et al. [91] fabricated carbon dots based on quinoline derivatives. These carbon sources were selected in order to improve PS's selectivity by targeting the nucleic acid of cells. In vitro PDT experiments were conducted in MCF-7 cells irradiated at 630 nm. Carbon dots were found to be biocompatible and to have sufficient penetration into the examined cells. Quantum yield of singlet oxygen was calculated to be 2.0%, 1.70%, and 2.9% depending on the used quinolone derivative. The synthesized PSs were tested in vivo after being conjugated with folic acid in order to improve selectivity for tumor tissue. Inhibition of tumor growth was achieved, while major organs such as the heart, liver, and kidney showed physiological morphological abnormalities.

In some other studies the synergistic effect of carbon-based quantum with known PSs was examined for the treatment breast cancer. In the research of Matshitse et al. [122], CQDs and GrQDs were conjugated with the phthalocyanine (2,9,16,23-tetrakis[4-(N-methylpyridyloxy)] (ZnTPPcQ). The synthesis of desired nanomaterials was carried out by means of chemical oxidation of graphene oxide sheets with potassium permanganate and sulfuric acid. Both CQDs and GrQDs were non-covalently linked with ZnTPPcQ and their PDT effects were compared with those of correspondent conjugates of ZnTPPcQ with nanodiamonds. The existence of carbon-based quantum dots in the conjugates enhanced the cytotoxicity to MCF-7 cells upon irradiation at 686 nm compared with ZnTPPcQ alone. The conjugation of ZnTPPcQ with CQDs and nanodiamonds resulted in a higher singlet oxygen quantum yield compared with ZnTPPcQ alone, while GrQDs maintain the singlet oxygen quantum yield of ZnTPPcQ. Likewise, Nene et al. [123] investigated the PDT effect of GrQDs’ conjugates with neutral and cationic morpholine substituted phthalocyanines. The conjugates showed reduced PDT efficiency compared to free phthalocyanines. This observation is supplementary to the reduced singlet oxygen quantum yield of conjugated phthalocyanines compared with free ones. It has been observed that when the conjugates were linked with biotin, cellular uptake was enhanced and the levels of cell viability appeared to be lower compared to free phthalocyanines. This issue can be explained by the abundance of biotin receptors in cancer cells. The conjugates with cationic phthalocyanine exhibited higher PDT efficiency due to the electrostatic interaction with the anionic cell membrane. Except for the phthalocyanines, porphyrin derivatives have also been tested as potential PSs combined with carbon-based quantum dots. Specifically, the porphyrin derivatives used were TMPP, ZnTMPP, GaClTMPP, and their conjugates with GrQDs were biocompatible with the cells and stable due to strong π-π stacking interaction. In the presence of GrQDs, porphyrin derivatives showed improved singlet oxygen quantum yield. GrQDs offer electron-donating groups and enhanced intersystem crossing. As a result, GrQDs improved the PDT efficiency of the porphyrins, and the GaClTMPP/GrQDs conjugate showed the lowest cell viability of 15% at a concentration of 120 μg/ml compared to the other conjugates [124].

In some studies, the interest is focused on the combination of PDT and chemotherapy in MCF-7 cells treatment, and thus carbon-based quantum dots have been tested as delivery vehicles of chemotherapeutic drugs. Yao et al. [125] demonstrated the nanoplatform DOX/Met/BSA-HA-CDs as a solution to tumor hypoxia. In this research work, carbon dots were conjugated with the antihyperglycaemic agent Metformin to enhance the oxygen supply and with hyaluronic acid (HA) to target the MCF-7 cells. Bovine serum albumin (BSA) is used to facilitate drug delivery. Carbon dots fluoresce in the red region of the spectrum, making deeper tissue penetration easier, while exhibiting superb biocompatibility. The proposed material improved PDT efficiency upon irradiation at 532 nm and has the ability to release gradually the drug. Firstly, Met is released to secure the required oxygen amount, and afterwards DOX is released continuously in a pH-dependent way. The PDT performance of the nanoplatform was also evaluated in vivo, and tumor suppression was achieved in mice with insignificant side effects. Apoptosis was the main cellular death mechanism observed in this case. In study reported by Khodadadei et al. [126], the chemotherapeutic drug Methotrexate (MTX) was successfully delivered in MCF-7 cells by using GrQDs (see Fig. 30 ). In this case, N-GrQDs were hydrothermally prepared with citric acid and urea, and MTX was loaded onto N-GrQDs via the π-π stacking interaction. The PDT efficiency of the drug delivery system N-GrQDs-MTX depends on the incubation time. For instance, MTX results in lower cell viability after 12 h of incubation than N-GrQDs/MTX, while 24 h of incubation of N-GrQDs/MTX is more PDT efficient than MTX itself. This indicates that loaded MTX requires more time to diffuse into the cells compared with free MTX.

A lot of research has been done on the elimination of 4T1 cells, which represent the breast cancer cell line of a mouse. Rare-earth doped upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) conjugated with GrQDs were used as PS agents against 4T1 cells by Zhang et al. [127]. UCNPs are selected due to their ability to emit UV light when excited by NIR light. The synthesized PS targeted mitochondria by modification with the fluorescent probe Tetramethylrhodamine-5-isothiocyanate (TRITC) in order to secure rapid cell apoptosis, which was the prominent cell death mechanism. The generation of cytotoxic singlet oxygen specifically in mitochondria resulted in instant mitochondrial damage, activation of caspase, and finally the initiation of the cellular death path of apoptosis. Citric acid and urea were the precursors for the hydrothermal synthesis of GrQDs, which were conjugated via amide linkages with UCNPs. Upon 980 nm light irradiation, mitochondria-targeting UCNP-GrQD/TRITC agent showed enhanced biocompatibility and PDT efficiency compared to non-targeting ones. In vivo experiments proved that nanomaterials preferably accumulate long-term in tumor sites than in major organs due to the EPR effect. Also, blood circulation studies revealed that the covalent conjugation of nanoparticles with other components improved blood circulation stability. Pathological insult in major organs was not evident with the use of the proposed nanoplatform, and the mouse group treated with the nanoplatform showed more apoptotic cells. These cells were pyknotic with condensed nuclei. In another study reported by Yang et al. [128], GrQDs were loaded into the cavities of hollow mesoporous silica nanoparticles (hMSN), in order to inhibit the rapid clearance of GrQDs from the renal system. Polythiophene (PT2) was the precursor used to synthesize hydrothermally the GrQDs, and the fabricated nanoparticles were further functionalized with PEG. The nanosystem exhibited great biocompatibility and reduced viability of 4T1 cells after irradiation. Singlet oxygen quantum yields for GrQDs and GrQDs@hMSN-PEG were calculated to be 1.2 and 0.9, respectively. In vivo experiments were also conducted, proving that the main organs, including the kidney, liver, and heart, showed no significant changes even after 30 days of injection. Biochemical and blood analyses were carried out, resulting in the conclusion that the proposed PSs caused no obvious toxicity. Furthermore, GrQDs@hMSN-PEG has a high drug loading capacity for DOX, which is released in a pH-dependent way.

The importance of the tumor microenvironment in cancer's progression and metastasis is highlighted in the study of Sun et al. [129] The synthesized PS consisted of carbon dots, the known PS Ce6, and copper ions. The ions are selected due to their ability to consume glutathione (GSH), that collects ROS and inhibits PDT efficiency. The second role of copper ions is to react with H2O2, which is abundant in cancer cells, to produce free radicals. Carbon dots were produced in a solvothermal process by citric acid, polyethylenimine (PEI), and formamide. The tumor microenvironment stimulates the action of Cu-CDs, and this PS ensures the deep penetration and quick clearance from the body. Cu-CDs/Ce6 complex can selectively accumulate and act in tumor cells more than in normal ones as a result of enhanced ∙OH production via Fenton-like reactions and glutathione depletion under the tumor microenvironment's specific conditions. The photodynamic action of this complex was assessed in 4T1 cells upon irradiation at 660 nm. A hemolysis assay was carried out in order to examine the complex safety. The proposed PS is highly hemocompatible, as no obvious color of red blood cells was evident in various PS concentrations. In vivo experiments proved successful tumor suppression and confirmed the absence of toxicity in major organs. Additionally, cell necrosis and apoptosis are the main cellular death mechanisms in this case. The advantages of copper ions in PDT were also recently utilized in the synthesis of a drug delivery system based on arginine-silver-copper co-doped CQDs. The addition of a silver dopant is desirable to improve the fluorescence properties of CQDs and enhance the generation of reactive nitrogen species (RNS). These features lead to the inhibition of tumor progress. CQDs were hydrothermally synthesized using l-arginine, silver nitrate (AgNO3) and copper nitrate (Cu(NO3)2). Water solubility and biocompatibility of CQDs are the ideal characteristics to permit their use as vehicles for the anticancer drug DOX. The selectivity of the as-synthesized PS to cancer cells was confirmed by comparison with the normal cell line HUVEC. Cancer cell viability declined at 50% at PS's concentration 200 μg/mL, while the normal cells remain almost intact. This observation can be explained by the generation of ROS and RNS in the tumor microenvironment. ROS production was further tested with 660 nm light irradiation. The synthesized PS induces cell apoptosis as a cell death mechanism, and caspase 3 was expressed while the expression of the anti-apoptotic proteins Bcl-2 was reduced as a result of efficient PDT action. Hemolysis assay was conducted and confirmed the superb biocompatibility of the proposed nanomaterials. In vivo performance in mice with breast cancer lung metastasis proved dual therapeutic PDT/CDT action with minimal side effects (see Fig. 31 ), as there was no toxicity reported in major organs [130].

Tumor hypoxia constitutes a limitation in PDT efficiency and various studies deploy the characteristics of the tumor microenvironment to develop PSs capable of elevating oxygen levels. Chen et al. [131] presented the utilization of the enzyme catalase to produce oxygen from abundant H2O2 in cancer cells. The enzyme is immobilized on nanospheres, which contain N-GrQDs and PPIX and are coated with the membrane of 4T1 cells. This system provides enhanced selectivity due to cell membrane coating, improved oxygen supply to the tumor, and sufficient oxygen to activate the hydrothermally synthesized N-GrQDs and PPIX to act as PSs upon 630 nm laser irradiation. The hemocompatibility of the proposed PS was also evaluated and was lower than 5% at various PS concentrations. This indicates that the nanomaterials are safe to be used in biomedical applications. Selective accumulation in tumor tissue was confirmed via ex vivo fluorescence imaging, while no organ abnormalities were reported. Another way to secure a sufficient oxygen supply is to use water-splitting materials. Zheng et al. [132] presented such nanoplatform that is based on CQDs. Particularly, CQDs were prepared hydrothermally with citric acid and urea and were further doped with carbon nitride (C3N4). Afterwards, carbon dots were conjugated with a polymer containing PPIX, PEG, and a targeting Arg-Gly-Asp (RGD) sequence. The nanoplatform exhibited similar PDT efficiency in 4T1 cells in hypoxic and normoxic medium, indicating the ability to produce enhanced ROS in a limited oxygen tumor microenvironment. In-vivo anticancer therapy revealed that PS accumulates mainly in the tumor and causes significant tumor suppression.

In similar research reported by Zhang et al. [133] C3N4 was also used to coat silica nanospheres, which contain N-GrQDs and were functionalized with the polymer P-PEG-RGD (hematoporphyrin derivative photofrin-PEG-peptide RGD). This photosensitizer was tested in 4T1 cells upon 630 nm irradiation and showed significant ROS production and oxygenated tumor. In addition to this, preferable accumulation in tumors was reported, and a fluorescence signal in the kidney can be attributed to renal excretion of the PS. The synthesized materials can also be applied as potential ultrasound contrast agents due to their generation of considerable echogenic effects. In this case, apoptosis and necrosis are the following cellular death pathways, while histological analysis of the major organs did not reveal any abnormalities.

The PDT efficiency of GrQDs was also evaluated in the MDA-MB-231 breast cancer cell line. In this case, N-GrQDs were hydrothermally prepared with citric acid and dicyandiamide and afterwards they were conjugated with 3-(aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES). The nanosystem was utilized to deliver DOX and the APTES secures increased nucleus targeting and generally, enhances the stability of the drug. When the concentration of the PS N-GrQDs/DOX/APTES is greater than 5 μg/mL, the proposed PS is more PDT efficient than DOX itself upon 622 nm laser irradiation [134]. In recent study reported by Mangalath et al. [135], GrQDs were conjugated with the BODIPY derivative BDPA to form a PS agent for MDA-MB-231 destruction. Chemical oxidation of GO with nitric acid was the synthesis route of GrQDs, which were conjugated via 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-carbodiimide-N-hydroxysuccini-mide coupling with BDPA. Singlet oxygen quantum yields of BDPA and GrQDs-BDPA were defined at 85% and 91%, respectively. The singlet oxygen generation ability of the GrQDs-BDPA is attributed to the BODIPY derivative, as GrQDs do not generate singlet oxygen under similar experimental conditions. The GrQDs/BDPA had improved water solubility, singlet oxygen production, and PDT efficiency compared with free BDPA. In addition to this, GrQDs-BDPA initiated apoptotic cell death to a higher extent than GrQDs and BDPA alone. A photodynamic combined phototherapy approach was used to treat MDA-MB-231 cells with GrQDs. This study presents a green way to synthesize hydrothermally GrQDs by using withered leaves of the Indian fig tree Ficus racemosa. GrQDs acted as dual photothermal and photodynamic agent, inducing apoptosis cell death. The cell imaging after treatment was also studied. In Fig. 32 GrQDs-labelled cancer cells, taken by using confocal laser scanning microscope, can be seen. The morphology of cells changed due to hyperthermia and ROS after 12 h of treatment.
