After 18 h, cells have a round morphology, and after 24 h, only cell debris is visible. The existence of GrQDs 24 h of laser treatment after cell death indicates their high photostability [136].

Another drug delivery system for PDT based on CQDs was proposed by Pandey et al. [137]. The focus of this research work was the delivery of the therapeutic agent MTX to treat Breast Cancer Stem Cells (BCSC). One characteristic of this research is the synthesis of mesoporous CQDs that are uniform in size and shape and not hydrophobic as other porous materials. Citric acid, poly (ethylene glycol)-2-amino ethyl ether, and cetyl-trimethylammonium bromide were the precursors for synthesis of CQDs. It must be mentioned that folic acid was also added to enhance selectivity for cancer cells. This system was tested for its photodynamic and photothermal capabilities, resulting in the successful selective delivery of MTX to cancer tissue. The hemolysis assay revealed reduced hemolysis activity of the nanoplatform compared with free MTX or CQDs as a result of reduced charged functional groups. This fact inhibits the interaction with red blood cells. In vivo biodistribution was also studied, showing lower levels of the nanoagent in the spleen, liver, stomach, and brain.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon-based quantum dots in treatment of cervical cancer with PDT
ce:section.ce:para: PDT is used to treat cervical cancer as an alternative therapeutic procedure in order to avoid surgery and secure the fertility of patients. However, known PSs such as Photofrin cause photosensitivity. This increases the need to synthesize innovative PSs, which can be localized to selected tumor tissue and excreted quickly from the body. HeLa cells are the most commonly used cell line to represent cervical cancer in research experiments [168].

HeLa cells can be treated with GrQDs alone as PS through multistate sensitization. In this case, the required energy to produce ROS can be secured through the excited singlet state to the triplet one and then from the triplet state to the ground state of GrQDs. This double transition mechanism improves the production of ROS compared with the typical single transition, which is followed by the conventional PSs. The GrQDs were fabricated hydrothermally with polythiophene (PT2) as a precursor and had high QY, superior 1O2 production, and good biocompatibility. Treated cells with GrQDs showed shrinkage and blebs were formed. The PDT efficiency of GrQDs was compared with that of PPIX, resulting in 20% cell viability in the case of GrQDs and 35% for the same concentration of PPIX [138].

Some other studies highlight the improved results of known PSs, when they are conjugated with carbon-based quantum dots. CQDs can be conjugated with tetraplatinated porphyrin (PtPor), constituting an alternative PS for PDT. To explain it more, the new complex is developed due to the electrostatic reaction of PtPor and negatively charged carbon dots (see Fig. 33 ). Carbon dots were synthesized hydrothermally using citric acid and ethylenediamine. The main goal of this theranostic agent is to combine the optical properties of carbon dots with the antitumor properties of PtPor. Carbon dots transfer energy in PtPor through the FRET process. In parallel, the EPR effect is enhanced, due to the existence of carbon dots, and thus this innovative PS protects more normal tissues as this composite is also stable in the cellular environment. In addition, the CQDs-PtPor complex exhibits higher PDT efficacy than that of PtPor alone. This could be justified by the fact that carbon dots enhance the production of reactive oxygen species. Applying this PS to treat HeLa cells upon irradiation at 405 nm is evident that the carbon dots-PtPor complex exhibits the most efficient reduction in tumor cells viability [139].

Another example is delivering the hydrophobic boron dipyrromethene (BODIPY) into the hydrophilic cellular environment with the aid of hydrophilic carbon dots. In particular, carbon dots synthesized by dopamine and o-phenylenediamine can be conjugated with BODIPY in order to destroy HeLa cells. The PDT efficiency of CQDs/BODIPY (CBNPs) is compared with the correspondent efficiency of CQDs alone and BODIPY nanoparticles (BNPs) upon light irradiation at 625 nm. All tested PSs are not cytotoxic in the dark, and CBNPs have the highest cytotoxic effect upon irradiation, as can be confirmed by MTT and live/dead assay. Also, apoptosis was the prominent cellular death path [140].

As it has been already mentioned, PDT efficiency highly depends on the amount of oxygen in cancer tissue, and therefore because of hypoxia of the tumor microenvironment researchers are obliged to develop PSs capable of overcoming this limitation. Yang et al. [141] suggested as solution the water-splitting process. In this case, the selected PS can transform water into H2O2 and then into diatomic oxygen. The nanocomposite CDs/TiO2 nanotubes can serve this purpose, and the viability of HeLa cells is decreased up to 20% upon 650 nm light irradiation. The increasing fluorescence signal of CDs with the increase in incubation time indicates sufficient cell uptake ability. Also, a hemolysis assay was performed and showed great stability of the examined PS in blood. This result contributed to the in vivo application of the CDs/TiO2 nanocomposite. Tumor inhibition growth was achieved, while the nanocomposite was detained in major organs of the metabolic system, indicating its tendency to be excreted. In addition to this, the proposed PS is gradually cleared as the amount of Ti in the metabolic system organs decreased over 1 week. Another option is to design PSs capable of generating oxygen in situ, making use of high levels of H2O2 in the tumor microenvironment. The study by Jia et al. [142] presents a PS for generating oxygen in situ based on manganese carbon dots (Mn-CDs). Manganese phthalocyanine (Mn-Pc) is the main precursor in the synthesis of Mn-CDs and their surfaces are passivated with DSPE-PEG. The role of manganese is also to act as a contrast agent for magnetic resonance imaging. The quantum yield of singlet oxygen was calculated to be 0.4. The synthesized CDs are biocompatible, and the in vitro PDT experiments are conducted in HeLa cells with 635 nm light irradiation and administration of H2O2 to the culture medium, in order to simulate the tumor microenvironment. Mn-CDs were efficient PDT agents in hypoxic, mildly acidic, and abundant in H2O2 microenvironment. In vivo distribution of Mn-CDs showed that the PS is accumulated in tumors after 6 h of injection, and the liver and lung showed high fluorescence signals as a result of macrophage uptake in the reticuloendothelial system. The examined PS was totally excreted after 14 days of injection. Mn-CQDs with irradiation can treat tumor-bearing mice efficiently with minimal side effects, overcoming tumor hypoxia.

Doping agents are of the utmost importance for carbon-based quantum dots because doped CQDs and GrQDs exhibit improved PL. Consequently, many studies present the use of various doping agents in the synthesis of carbon dots in order to improve their PDT performance. For instance, as like as in work reported by Jia et al. [142], manganese has been also used by Irmania et al. [143] as a dopant in the green synthesis of CQDs using waste green tea. In this research, CQDs were synthesized hydrothermally using waste green tea and MnCl2 as the manganese dopant. The option of manganese ensures, as previously, the need for magnetic resonance imaging. The CQDs are further functionalized with amine groups by ethylenediamine (EDA). The PDT effect is achieved synergistically by conjugating Mn-CDs with Ce6. The nanoplatform also consists of folic acid (FA), whose receptors are overexpressed in cancer cells. The synthesized PS Mn-CQDs@FA/Ce6 was tested in vitro in HeLa cells, HepG2 cells (hepatocellular cancer cells), and B16F10 cells (melanoma cancer cells) upon irradiation at 671 nm. The lowest cell viability was observed in HeLa cells, and this behavior can be explained by the high affinity of HeLa cells for folic acid due to overexpression of folate receptors compared with the other tested cell lines. Also, the PS Mn-CQDs@FA/Ce6 does not present dark toxicity in all tested cell lines.

An alternative doping agent for the synthesis of CDs is copper. This metal is evident in traces in the human body, and the challenge is to use copper in the structure of CDs in order to improve PDT efficiency without altering CQDs’ fluorescence properties. In research work of Wang et al. [144], copper doped CDs (Cu-CDs) were synthesized using poly (acrylic acid) and copper nitrate as main precursors. The photodynamic action of Cu-CDs was investigated in HeLa cells upon laser irradiation at 400–700 nm. This innovative PS can be used in PDT and for diagnostic purposes due to its biocompatibility, high singlet oxygen production, and fluorescence properties. Regarding the singlet oxygen production of Cu-CDs, it is worth mentioning that Cu-CDs produce singlet oxygen with a quantum yield of 0.36. This quantum yield is comparable to that of common PSs in aqueous media and higher than that of CDs alone. Further application of copper doping is reported in the research work of Liu et al. [92]. Copper can detect biothiols, such as GSH, and it can, therefore, support in tumor diagnosis when biothiol levels are elevated. Copper ions were rich in chlorophyll due to their synthesis of spinach extracts and they were conjugated with CQDs. Apart from diagnostics, this PS can also be used for treatment, since copper consumes biothiol and reduces the energy level difference between chlorophyll molecules, leading thus to improved ROS production. Both CQDs and CQDs-Cu conjugates exhibited a PDT effect in HeLa cells upon light irradiation at 660 nm. The proposed nanoagent was employed in vivo fluorescence imaging of biothiol in mice, and the fluorescence signal in tumor sites was much higher than in normal ones. This confirmed the potential use of CQDs-Cu in tumor diagnosis, as intense fluorescence intensity indicates elevated levels of biothiol in tumor sites. CQDs-Cu could treat tumors in vivo, and after 12 days of treatment, tumor tissues disappeared.

Nitrogen and sulfur have been studied as possible doping agents in the synthesis of PSs based on carbon-based quantum dots. Jovanovic et al. [145] fabricated N-GrQDs and N, S co-doped GrQDs electrochemically using graphite electrodes and urea as well as thiourea as precursors for modification. EPR spectroscopy revealed that N,S co-doped GrQDs were unable to produce singlet oxygen, whereas N-GrQDs improved the 1O2 production of pure GrQDs. N, S co-doped GrQDs showed significant antioxidative activity by quenching 86% ∙OH at a concentration of 10 μg/mL. Both pure GrQDs and N-GrQDs were not toxic to the normal cell line MRC-4, and pure GrQDs showed negligible toxicity towards HeLa cells up to 300 μg/ml concentration. In contrast to pure GrQDs, N-GrQDs can be used as PS agents in HeLa cell treatment up to a concentration of 100 μg/mL (see Fig. 34 ). Milenkovic et al. [146] applied gamma irradiation to modify N, S co-doped GrQDs in various irradiation doses and used them as potential PS agents in HeLa cells. GrQDs were synthesized via electrochemical oxidation of graphite and functionalized with isopropyl alcohol and l-cysteine (N, S dopant) with the aid of gamma irradiation. The utilization of gamma irradiation improved the QY of GrQDs compared with pure ones, and the results were confirmed by means of fluorescence microscopy. The GrQDs with an irradiation dose at 25 kGy showed great visibility of HeLa cells, had the highest QY and the ability to produce singlet oxygen based on EPR spectroscopy.

The importance of monitoring organelles in PDT treatment is highlighted in the research work published recently by Yi et al. [147]. This study proposes precisely monitoring of nucleolus since a PS targeted at the nucleolus can inhibit the growth of cancer cells. Using p-aminoazobenzene (PAB) as a precursor, carbon dots can be used to synthesize the desired PS. The synthesized CDs can emit at 605 nm, and N doping of CDs has a vital role in nucleolus targeting. In vitro PDT experiments were conducted in HeLa cells irradiated at 638 nm, and the main features of the PS were no dark toxicity and efficient photodynamic action. Histopathological examination showed no acute or chronic pathological damage in major organs after 14 days when mice were injected with the proposed CDs. In Fig. 35 , nucleolus targeting is obvious due to strong red fluorescence without irradiation, and the production of ROS quenches the fluorescence gradually with irradiation at 638 nm. Another organelle to monitor is the lysosome, in order to achieve PDT-combined photothermal therapy (PTT) treatment. The lysosome is responsible for maintaining cellular activity and stability, and the combination of these two therapies has the potential to destroy tumors through the generation of ROS or heat. This concept was studied in HeLa cells by using CQDs as PSs. Carbon dots were synthesized hydrothermally by trinitropyrene and had the ability to convert light energy into heat and produce ROS under 635 nm light irradiation. The proposed nanomaterials could produce singlet oxygen with a quantum yield of 5.7%. The synergistic effect of PDT and PTT was evident because HeLa cells’ viability reached 10% when treated with the examined CDs. Individual action of PDT and PTT concluded in cell viability of 80% and 50%, respectively [148].

A different perspective for improving PDT efficiency in HeLa cells is presented by Teng et al. [149]. This research focuses on the synthesis of probes capable of monitoring the generation of singlet oxygen in order to evaluate the PS and optimize the treatment conditions. Since biocompatibility and water solubility is required, carbon dots appear to be ideal candidates for the above purpose. Consequently, carbon dots were synthesized hydrothermally by histidine and were functionalized with imidazole rings, that can react with the generated 1O2 to form a structure with intense luminescence. The hemolysis assay showed insignificant changes in hemolysis percentage, indicating that the examined CDs were safe for live cells. In addition to this, examination of major organs revealed no damage after exposure to the CDs.

The variation of GSH levels in tumors and normal cells has been utilized to develop a PS for HeLa cells with high selectivity. The PS is based on a GrQDs-Ce6 conjugated system and can be activated selectively in tumor cells. In this work, GrQDs were synthesized by chemical oxidation of graphite and were functionalized with PEG via disulfide linkage in order to inhibit the quenching effect. In the case of a cancer cell, the disulfide linkage is cleaved and Ce6 is released to destroy the cell. This nanocarrier is significantly more toxic to HeLa cells than the free Ce6 upon 650 nm laser irradiation and can protect the normal cells from destruction due to GSH dependency. In vivo and ex vivo optical imaging was employed to study the GrQDs-PEG-Ce6 in comparison with the widely used GO-PEG-Ce6. The studies revealed that GrQDs-PEG-Ce6 exhibited higher tumor uptake and an increased fluorescence signal after 1 h of intravenous injection. It is important to note that GrQDs-PEG-Ce6 was quickly excreted as fluorescence signal lowered with increasing time. This observation is complementary with the main accumulation of GrQDs-PEG-Ce6 in the kidney [150]. In another study, negatively charged MnO2 nanosheets were used as detectors of GSH and were adsorbed on the surface of positively charged GrQDs. PT2 was the carbon precursor for the hydrothermal synthesis of GrQDs. Due to energy transfer, MnO2 quenches the fluorescence of GrQDs. However, MnO2 nanosheets are decomposed in the presence of GSH, and thus GrQDs’ fluorescence is enhanced. This mechanism aims to consume the GSH in cancer cells with the reduction of MnO2 to Mn2+ and therefore improves the PDT efficiency because GSH does not reduce the produced ROS [151].

The synergistic effect of PDT and chemotherapy is also being investigated as a potential solution for cervical cancer. Chiu et al. [113] developed a PS based on tri-element doped CQDs to deliver DOX. Microwave pyrolysis was applied to produce gadolinium, nitrogen, and sulfur co-doped CQDs (GdNS@CQDs). The selectivity of the nanocarrier was enhanced with its conjugation with folic acid, while HeLa and HepG2 (human liver carcinoma cells) were the tested cell lines. FA-GdNS@CQDs-DOX was more efficient in HeLa cells treatment than free DOX and the abundance of folate receptors in HeLa cells constitute the proposed therapeutic agent more efficient in HeLa than HepG2 cells. In vivo imaging and tissue distribution of FA-GdNS@CQDs were also examined in zebrafish in order to test their potential use in vivo in biomedical applications. FA-GdNS@CQDs were evident in various locations of zebrafish, and their small size and enhanced water solubility make them easily absorbable and ingestible. Fluorescence signals were almost disappeared at the 7th day, indicating a nanoagent with fast excretion and decreased possibility of long-term toxicity. Dong et al. [152] presented a nanoplatform to deliver DOX based on carbon-based quantum dots, which were encapsulated in hMCN. In this study, PT2 was the precursor to preparing hydrothermally GrQDs, and DOX was also loaded on the platform along with PEG functionalization. Cell viability in the case of GrQDs alone does not differentiate from that of GrQDs/hMCN and GrQDs/hMCN-PEG. The quantum yield of singlet oxygen production was calculated to be 0.66 and 0.47 for GrQDs and GrQDs/hMSN, respectively. However, the DOX loading on GrQDs/hMCN-PEG proved a significant reduction in cell viability. All the tested samples showed cell viability above 80% without irradiation. Significant tumor suppression was recorded in the cases of GrQDs/hMSN-PEG and GrQDs/hMSN (DOX)-PEG upon laser irradiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon-based quantum dots in treatment of lung cancer with PDT
ce:section.ce:para: PDT has been used as a palliation strategy in patients with advanced lung cancer and as a therapeutic approach for early-stage lung cancer with inoperable tumors [169,170]. In particular, the development of laparoscopic light delivery systems gives a new perspective to the treatment of lung cancer with PDT, as the area of interest can be easily accessible [171]. PDT is considered a safe treatment procedure for relieving airway obstruction in case of inoperable cancer [172,173]. In addition to this, PDT can be effective in lung cancer patients when combined with chemotherapy. PDT intends to regulate internal bronchial tumor growth, while chemotherapy aims to improve survival in patients with advanced lung cancer [173].

A recent study reported by He et al. [153], examines the photodynamic action of a photocatalyst, consisting of CQDs and the semiconductor Bi4O5Br2. CQDs were synthesized hydrothermally using d-Glucose and ethylenediamine. PDT was conducted in A549 cell line upon UV light controlling irradiation time and PS concentration. An increase of these two parameters leads to enhanced PDT efficiency and the morphological structure of cancer cells was depicted with the aid of an inverted fluorescence microscope. The synthesized composites exhibit reduced cancer cell viability, low cytotoxicity, enhanced intracellular ROS production, and easier penetration into the cell's membrane compared with Bi4O5Br2 itself. The last observation can be attributed to the hydrophilic group on the CQDs' surface. In this study, CQDs/Bi4O5Br2 initiates apoptotic cell death, as the proposed PS with the aid of irradiation significantly increased the apoptosis rate.

Zhao et al. [154] proposed the synthesis of N, P co-doped carbon dots (R-CDs) and N doped carbon dots (Y-CDs) as potent bioimaging and photosensitizing agents for the A549 cell line. Both types of CDs were synthesized hydrothermally using o-Phenylenediamine as a precursor, and especially R-CDs consisted of phosphoric acid as a phosphorus agent. The as-synthesized CDs exhibit no dark toxicity in the examined cell line. Furthermore, R-CDs showed the lowest cell viability rates upon 532 nm laser irradiation. This feature led to the conduction of in vivo PDT experiments in mice with lung tumor, which proved the successful use of N, P co-doped CDs in PDT. Apoptosis was the main cell death mechanism in this case. Also, these PSs can be considered safe due to low uptake by various organs such as the liver, kidney, and heart.

GrQDs have also been investigated as potential therapeutic agents for lung cancer. In the study of Nafiujjaman et al. [155] the conjugation of GrQDs with Ce6 as PS for the A549 cell line was presented. GrQDs were synthesized by chemical oxidation of carbon fibers and Ce6 was further conjugated with HA due to overexpression of HA-sensitive CD44 receptors on cancer cells. At 670 nm laser irradiation, GrQDs-HA-Ce6 exhibited superior PDT efficacy in A549 cells with 82% cell death compared with 50% cell death in the same concentration of free Ce6. GrQDs can also be used as PSs in lung cancer treatment with the combination of PDT and PTT. Namely, GrQDs were synthesized via chemical oxidation of GO and were modified with PEG and aptamer. Afterwards, GrQDs were conjugated with porphyrin derivatives (P). The quantum yields of singlet oxygen production from P and GrQDs-PEG-P were calculated to be 1.27 and 1.08, respectively. This difference did not affect the final PDT performance, as energy resonance transfer limits the influence of GrQDs on P. It has been found that the resultant PS can act as an intracellular cancer-related microRNA (miRNA) biomarker detector. The viability of A549 cells decreased up to 14% after irradiation with 635 nm and 980 nm combined irradiation, indicating the effectiveness of PDT/PTT compared with PDT or PTT alone. Apoptosis is the prominent cell death mechanism [156].

The synergistic effect of PDT, PTT, and chemotherapy was studied in the HBE lung cancer cell line. Hydrothermal method was used to prepare CQDs with citric acid and polyglutamic acid, which were further modified with Fe-crosslinked carboxymethyl chitin complex (Fe-CMC). The Fe3O4@CQDs were attached to PEGylated SWCNTs and the nanocarrier SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4@CQDs was used to deliver DOX. Improved cellular uptake capability was secured via the conjugation with a sgc8c aptamer. The tri-modal PDT/PTT/chemotherapy efficacy was proven in both HBE and HeLa cells under irradiation at 808 nm (2W/cm2), resulting in cell death of up to 95% (see Fig. 36 ). Hemolysis assay showed that the nanocarrier is safe and can hinder bleeding during injection of the carrier compared with DOX. This was proved by the low hemolysis rate of the nanocarrier, which indicates no significant hemolytic activity on red blood cells. The use of this PS did not damage any major organs such as the lung, liver, heart, kidney, and spleen, according to data after 14 and 28 days of the treatment [157].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon-based quantum dots in treatment of oral cancer with PDT
ce:section.ce:para: Oral tumors are superficial lesions, and this facilitates the use of PDT as a therapeutic approach because light can penetrate the oral cavity more easily. Li et al. [158] proposed the use of sulfur-doped carbon dots (S-CDs) to treat UM1 cells. This cell line refers to squamous cell carcinoma of the oral tongue. PT2 was the precursor for the synthesis of S-CDs. The PDT efficacy of S-CDs in UM1 cells upon irradiation at 420 nm is higher than that of 5-aminolevulinic acid (5-ALA). The two examined PSs were further investigated in morphology changes with acridine orange (AO) and propidium iodide (PI) to depict live (green) and dead (red) cells, respectively. S-CDs lead to drastic changes in cell morphology, whereas 5-ALA exhibits no significant changes compared to control group (see Fig. 37 ). Also, S-CDs have low dark toxicity and improved expression of proteins associated with apoptosis, such as caspase-3 and Bax proteins. On the other hand, downregulation of the Bcl-2 protein was evident, and this constituted one more confirmation about PDT-mediated cell apoptosis.

The use of CQDs in PDT was tested in another study in the H413 cell line, which is considered a squamous cell carcinoma of the oral cavity [159]. This research focuses on the synthesis of two types of carbon dots by curcumin (CDc) and curcumin/folic acid (CDcf). Curcumin is recognized as PS and folic acid acts as a targeting agent due to the overexpression of folate receptors in the cancer cells’ membrane. Both types of CDs were excited with near-infrared (NIR) light (λ = 780 nm) and targeted at the nucleus of H413 cells in order to improve the destruction of cancer cells. The existence of folic acid is crucial to PDT efficiency because CDcf exhibited increased ROS production and DNA damage in comparison with the corresponding measurements of CDc.

Zhang et al. [160] investigated the treatment of oral squamous cell carcinoma with GrQDs, which were linked with PEG. An in vitro study was conducted on SCC9 and SCC25 human cell lines and the proposed PS exhibited no significant dark toxicity in concentrations up to 100 μg/ml. Upon 560 nm light irradiation, SCC9 cells and SCC25 cells exhibit viability of 30% and 70%, respectively, when they are incubated with 100 μg/mL GrQDs/PEG. Apoptosis was initiated by the proposed PS as a cellular death path. In addition, endogenous tumor antigens were released after PDT, which activated CD8+ cytotoxic T cells, and this constitutes the GrQDs/PEG as an option to combine PDT and immunotherapy. In vivo optical imaging revealed the accumulation of GrQDs/PEG in tumor tissue, while the slow metabolism of GrQDs/PEG was proved according to fluorescence signals lasting up to 24 h. The examined PS was also tested in vivo, where a 70% reduction in tumor size was reported.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon-based quantum dots in treatment of brain cancer with PDT
ce:section.ce:para: Due to its high selectivity, PDT constitutes an alternative therapeutic method to treat brain tumors. To elaborate, PSs have the ability to localize in pathological areas of brain tissue while causing no harm to normal ones. The use of PDT to treat brain cancer is mainly recommended at early-stage and recurrent cancers [174]. Li et al. [161] used PDT to treat a glioblastoma cell line (U87-MG), which is a type of malignant brain cancer. S-CDs were fabricated hydrothermally using PT2 as a precursor. S-CDs could produce singlet oxygen with a quantum yield of 0.95. The photodynamic action of S-CDs was evaluated in comparison with the use of PT2 and Ce6 as PSs upon irradiation of cancer cells at 660 nm. PDT treatment of cells with S-CDs exhibited the lowest cell viability rates compared to the other PSs. In Fig. 38 , changes in the morphological structure of U87-MG cells after the PDT treatment with S-CDs can be seen. These changes in cells are identical to apoptosis, and S-CDs were investigated as a suppression agent of the PI3K/Akt signaling pathway that intensifies cancer cell proliferation. Expression of apoptosis proteins was further studied, showing downregulation of Bcl-2 and Bax proteins while cytochrome c was released.

Markovic et al. [162] investigated the use of GrQDs as potential PSs to treat human U251 glioma cancer cells. Electrochemical oxidation of graphite rods was the selected approach to synthesize GrQDs with superior PL properties. The cell viability decreased to 40% after irradiation at 470 nm and the cell's morphology changed to round and smaller from polygonal. PDT was attributed to apoptosis and autophagy, due to the appearance of related morphological and biochemical characteristics. To be more specific, apoptosis was confirmed by a flow cytometric analysis of cell size/granularity, as more round and smaller cells were already observed. Apoptotic-related enzymes such as caspases were activated through PDT. Autophagy is considered to be able to promote apoptotic and non-apoptotic cells. In this case, vacuolization of cells was evident when incubated with GrQDs after 12 h. Internalization of GrQDs may have occurred through endocytosis, as intracytoplasmic vesicles contained GrQDs. Vesicles also contained cellular components, including mitochondria, and this resembled the formation of autophagic vacuoles. The main role of autophagy is to clear the cell of the damaged organelles and proteins caused by PDT. The downregulation of the protein p62 by GrQDs was also a confirmation of the contribution of autophagy.

The synergistic effect of PDT and chemotherapy was investigated in pediatric brain tumor cells [163]. In this study, carbon dots were synthesized via chemical oxidation and were conjugated with the glycoprotein transferrin in order to allow DOX to cross the blood-brain barrier. The option of transferrin can be justified by the overexpression of transferrin receptors in cancer cells. This multifunctional nanoplatform was more PDT efficient in cell lines SJGBM2, CHLA266, CHLA200, and DAOY than free DOX at specific concentrations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon-based quantum dots in treatment of melanoma skin cancer with PDT
ce:section.ce:para: Melanoma is a malignant form of skin cancer. The main characteristics of melanoma are drug resistance, high death and recurrence rates. Treatment of melanoma with PDT is a challenge, especially in metastatic cases, due to the limited access of the lesion to light irradiation and melanin pigmentation. Research interests are focused on the synthesis of PSs with high affinity for target cancer tissue and an enhanced ability to absorb light at longer wavelengths in order to act on the selected deep tissue [175].

One use of carbon dots in skin cancer treatment with PDT is upon combination with Ce6. This PS is widely used in PDT due to its enhanced singlet oxygen yield, rapid clearance of the body, and bright fluorescence in the red region of the spectrum. However, its water solubility and specificity in tumor targeting is limited. However, with the conjugation of Ce6 with carbon dots such difficulties can be solved. FRET is again a means to transfer energy from carbon dots to Ce6. A new approach to this complex is to add polysaccharide HA, which is known for drug delivery, and HA's receptors are highly expressed in tumor cells. This PS can be used in PDT to deal with melanoma skin cancers, especially the B16F10 cell line. Carbon dots were synthesized by thermal decomposition using glycerin as main precursor. The effectiveness of this conjugate used in PDT can be seen in Fig. 39 , which depicts a live/dead assay with the aid of confocal microscopy. Live skin cancer cells exhibited green fluorescence. Furthermore, it was observed that the use of Ce6 or Cdot-Ce6 as PSs did not cause a serious declination of tumor cells compared to the control sample (PBS). On the other hand, it was obvious that the death of tumor cells occurred in the case of Cdot-Ce6-HA as PS, as it shows red fluorescence. Alternatively, the efficacy of the therapy can be evident with Fig. 39 evaluating not only the PSs, but the use of laser irradiation at 660 nm. Nanoconjugate accumulates in the tumor tissues of mice inoculated with B16F10 cells, as confocal microscopy revealed. Tumor inhibition growth was achieved with the use of Cdot-Ce6-HA and laser irradiation, while apoptosis and necrosis are the prominent cellular death mechanisms based on cell pyknosis [164].

Ahirwar et al. [165] suggested the application of GrQDs and Graphene Oxide Quantum Dots (GrOQDs) as potential PS for PDT treatment of the B16F10 melanoma cell line and the MCF-7 breast cancer cell line. Both GrQDs and GrOQDs were synthesized via electrochemical oxidation of graphite rods with differentiation in electrolyte solution. A UV tubelight was used as an irradiation source, and GrQDs and GrOQDs were biocompatible in the tested cell lines. In the case of GrOQDs, the abundance of functional groups offers more efficiency in singlet oxygen production. Generally, it has been found that less than 10% of cells were alive after treatment with GrQDs and GrOQDs upon 5 min of UV irradiation (see Fig. 40 ). However, the use of UV irradiation restricts the application to skin-related cancer due to limited penetration of this irradiation in deeper cancerous tissues.

An alternative option to treat skin cancers mainly with PDT is to couple carbon dots with PPIX, which is a conventional PS. Such combination deploys the FRET mechanism to excite PPIX indirectly with the contribution of carbon dots. In study reported by Cosme et al. [166], two different types of PPIX-CDs conjugates were synthesized based on different cross-linking techniques. To analyze this more, PPIX@CD refers to the encapsulation of PPIX into CDs, and PPIX-CD is synthesized based on amide cross-linking. Precursor compounds in the synthesis of CDs were citric acid and ethylenediamine. In vitro PDT experiments were conducted in the melanoma cell line C8161 upon laser irradiation at 412 nm. Both types of conjugates improve the therapeutic window of the PS since similar PDT effect can be achieved in lower concentrations of complexes compared to PPIX itself.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Overview of the use of carbon-based quantum dots in PDT
ce:section.ce:para: Recently, carbon-based quantum dots have been examined as PSs in various types of cancer such as breast, cervical, skin, oral etc. Taking into account the results of the presented studies, it is concluded that carbon-based quantum dots are valuable PDT agents and can also contribute to the development of nanoplatforms for tumor diagnosis. Water solubility, biocompatibility, and negligible toxicity are the most attractive properties of carbon-based quantum dots, which encourage their use as PSs alone or in conjugation with other commonly used PSs [14,16]. Carbon-based quantum dots are conjugated with common PSs via electrostatic interaction or/and π-π stacking and the addition of carbon-based quantum dots improves the PDT efficiency and water solubility of the PSs. Except for commonly used PSs, the synergistic PDT effect of carbon-based quantum dots with various nanoparticles, including doped up-conversion nanoparticles and hollow mesoporous silica nanoparticles, has also been investigated [127,128,152]. Furthermore, carbon-based quantum dots are examined as candidate agents for combinational PDT and chemotherapy treatment since CQDs/GrQDs are used to deliver chemotherapeutic drugs [113,125,126,157,163]. In other recent applications of CQDs/GrQDs in PDT, researchers exploit the special characteristics of each cancer type and develop the suitable photosensitizer. For instance, there is a tendency to bind the proposed PS with targeting molecules, such as biotin, folic acid, or hyaluronic acid [113,123,125,137,143,155,159,164]. These molecules are overexpressed in certain cancer types, and this enhances their selectivity in cancerous cells. Research studies presented the ability of CQDs to induce apoptosis, necrosis and autophagy as PDT agents. However, apoptosis is the prominent cellular death mechanism mediated by PDT.

Previous sections described the available methods to synthesize carbon-based quantum dots as well as some of their major features and their increasingly growing application for cancer treatment with PDT. However, there is a need to examine crucial carbon nanomaterials’ parameters such as synthesis method, precursor compounds, modification, particle size, and fluorescence and singlet oxygen quantum yield under the scope of recent PDT research. In addition to this, PDT action of carbon-based quantum dots was compared with that of commonly used PSs such as phthalocyanines, BODIPY, Ce6.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The role of synthesis route for carbon-based quantum dots used in PDT
ce:section.ce:para: In section 3, we thoroughly described the available synthetic paths for CQDs and GrQDs, along with the advantages and disadvantages of each method. However, the examination of the recent use of carbon-based quantum dots in PDT proves that some synthetic methods outbalance others. Particularly, hydrothermal treatment is the most common procedure to produce CQDs and GrQDs for PDT applications. The convenience of doping, the use of water as a solvent, and the avoidance of harsh chemicals facilitate the use of the hydrothermal method in carbon-based quantum dots synthesis. All these studies, following hydrothermal treatment, use an autoclave reactor to apply controlled temperature and pressure conditions to precursor compounds. On the other hand, the solvothermal method is not a widespread technique to obtain CQDs and GrQDs. Ethanol and formamide are the most commonly used alternate solvents to water. Electrochemical oxidation is another common way to synthesize primarily GrQDs for use in PDT. Graphite rods are widely used as electrodes, and NaOH/ethanol is used as an electrolyte. The option of electrochemical oxidation lies in its simple and economic way of producing the desirable nanomaterials [145,146]. The last common way to fabricate carbon-based quantum dots for PDT is chemical oxidation. The use of strong oxidizing acids and lack of control over carbon-based quantum dots' sizes seems not to affect nanomaterials’ PDT efficiency in cancer because several of the presented studies make use of this technique and highlight the important role of CQDs/GrQDs as PDT agents. GO sheets are the only used precursor for carbon-based quantum dot synthesis, and some other reagents for successful chemical oxidation are KMnO4, H2SO4, and HNO3 [122,135,150]. The procedure for CQDs/GrQDs synthesized with chemical oxidation involves neutralization with NaOH and filtration with a dialysis membrane to remove the excess salts [122,135]. The remaining studies fabricate carbon-based quantum dots based on microwave routes [113,166], reflux synthesis [125] and pyrolysis [144]. There are no recent reports regarding the production of CQDs/GrQDs for PDT using laser ablation or ultrasonic techniques as main strategies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The role of precursor compounds for carbon-based quantum dots used in PDT
ce:section.ce:para: The option of precursor compounds is vital for the chemical, optical, and physicochemical properties of carbon-based quantum dots, which have a major role in their success as PDT agents. The presented studies deploy various precursors depending on the selected synthesis method, the cancer type, or the molecules that are conjugated with CQDs or GrQDs. Citric acid constitutes the most popular carbon source for carbon-based quantum dots’ synthesis. Other commonly used carbon sources include d-Glucose, l-Arginine, histidine, polythiophene, dopamine, o-phenylenediamine, poly(acrylic acid), curcumin, glycerin, cysteine, and polyglutamic acid. Most of them also act as nitrogen, sulfur, or phosphorus doping agents. In the case of GrQDs, GO sheets constitute a popular option. Some studies present an alternative green CQDs/GrQDs synthesis by using waste green tea, spinach leaves, or withered leaves of the Indian fig tree. While others deploy the special features of the tumor microenvironment and choose manganese phthalocyanine or quinoline derivatives to face tumor hypoxia and target nucleic acid, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The role of carbon-based quantum dots’ modification in their use in PDT
ce:section.ce:para: Surface functionalization (passivation) and heteroatom doping are two popular strategies to tune the optical properties of carbon-based quantum dots. These two strategies are mainly responsible for carbon-based quantum dots' PL, which is vital for PDT [120]. The importance of CQDs and GrQDs modification is evident in the already discussed studies, which use these carbon nanomaterials for PDT. Surface modification of carbon-based quantum dots for PDT has been reported in several studies. Polyethylene glycol (PEG) and polyethyleneimine (PEI) are the most common surface passivation agents for carbon-based quantum dots used in PDT. According to literature, the role of these functionalization agents is major for PS's efficiency, as they improve biocompatibility, stability, aqueous solubility, fluorescence, singlet oxygen production, and minimize toxicity [128,137,157,176].

However, surface functionalization is considered a complicated procedure regarding purification processes and demands a selective choice of modification agents in order to use the produced materials in a biomedical application such as PDT. On the other hand, heteroatom doping seems to be a more efficient way to achieve the desirable features for carbon nanomaterials [177]. A lot of the presented studies make use of precursors with the aim of fabricating CQDs or GrQDs doped with nitrogen, phosphorus, sulfur, copper, or manganese. In some cases, the production of carbon-based quantum dots co-doped with nitrogen/sulfur, nitrogen/phosphorus, gadolinium/nitrogen/sulfur, or arginine/silver/copper is also reported. Nitrogen-doped carbon-based quantum dots are the most popular kind of doped CQDs/GrQDs used for PDT. The choice of nitrogen lies in its similar atomic radius and its larger electronegativity compared with the carbon atom. This makes nitrogen a facile element to be incorporated into the carbon network [145]. Based on the recent studies on CQDs/GrQDs application in PDT, urea is the most commonly used nitrogen source, while others make use of dicyandiamine, ethylenediamine, p-Aminoazobenzene, etc. Some other researchers apply dual heteroatom doping with nitrogen and sulfur or phosphorus in order to investigate further the potential use of co-doped CQDs/GrQDs in PDT. Regarding N, S co-doped GrQDs, they present no dark toxicity, although this kind of doped GrQDs are not efficient regarding singlet oxygen production in comparison with pristine GrQDs and N-doped GrQDs. Actually, N,S co-doped GrQDs have the tendency to quench OH radicals, hindering their application in PDT and there is a need to examine further their ability to quench other radical species [145,146]. Contrary to N,S co-doped GrQDs, N,P co-doped CQDs are high singlet oxygen producers and efficient in PDT against lung cancer cells. Co-doping with phosphorus offers an efficient way to adjust the optical properties of carbon dots, as phosphorus forms substitutional defects into the carbon network due to its larger size compared with the carbon atom [154]. In PDT research, manganese-doped CQDs have been recently applied to cervical cancer cells. Manganese has the potential to be an efficient agent for fluorescence and magnetic resonance (MR) imaging, allowing the combination of PDT with photothermal therapy (PTT) and the development of a nanoplatform for diagnostic and therapeutic purposes. In addition, manganese can be oxidized with the abundant H2O2 in cancer cells and generate oxygen in the hypoxic tumor microenvironment, which is important for PDT success [142,143]. Some other studies make use of copper as a doping agent in CQDs’ synthesis. Copper-doped CQDs exhibit biocompatibility and improved singlet oxygen production than pristine CQDs. Also, copper can decrease the levels of abundant biothiol in cancerous cells, and thus this leads to enhanced ROS production. Apart from the therapeutic action of copper in CQDs, it also reported the availability of tumor diagnosis, as Cu-CQDs can act as fluorescent probes for sensitive detection of biothiol [92,144].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The role of size distribution for carbon-based quantum dots used in PDT
ce:section.ce:para: The size of PS is also an important parameter for PDT success because larger particles have lower cell uptake and this constitutes an obstacle to the PDT effect [141,161]. Recent studies on the use of carbon-based quantum dots for PDT highlight the small size of these carbon nanomaterials and conduct Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) and Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) measurements in order to determine particle size and size distribution, respectively. Results showed that CQDs and GrQDs have a relatively narrow size distribution, which indicates uniformity in size. These results are valid for both pristine and doped carbon-based quantum dots. The average diameter of the synthesized carbon-based quantum dots is less than 10 nm, with a significant diameter increase observed in CQDs and GrQDs when they are conjugated with various biomolecules. These biomolecules include existing PSs (phthalocyanines, chlorins, porphyrin derivatives, BODIPY) or folic acid and hyaluronic acid. The rise in particle size is evident as a result of biomolecules stacking onto CQDs/GrQDs surface. It also constitutes an indication of the formation of conjugates. [122–124], 139] Additionally, heteroatom doping causes an increase in particle size compared with pristine CQDs/GrQDs. Some studies show that by binding CQDs with copper ions, the particle size can be changed from 2 nm to 20–180 nm depending on the concentration of copper ions [130]. This control can be useful in cases in which the particle size of the proposed PS is proportional to the retention time in the tumor site [130,152].

Another important aspect for successful cancer treatment with PDT is the optical properties of the PS, which in the case of carbon-based quantum dots are size-dependent. As a result of the quantum confinement effect, an increase in the carbon-based quantum dots’ size causes a reduction in the band gap. This leads to a red-shifted PL emission peak [86,178]. However, PL and ROS production are competing de-excitation pathways, and this means that an increased size for carbon-based quantum dots causes a decrease in ROS production [179].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The role of quantum yield for carbon-based quantum dots used in PDT
ce:section.ce:para: Enhanced fluorescence is a crucial feature of a proposed PS, as it enables its use as an agent for cell imaging and diagnosis [180]. PS's ability to convert absorbed light into emitted light in the form of fluorescence is quantified by the Quantum Yield (QY) [181]. Some of the recent applications of carbon-based quantum dots in PDT define the QY of pristine, doped or conjugated CQDs/GrQDs. The QY of various nano-photosensitizers (nano-PSs) is presented in Table 5 , along with the QY of carbon-based quantum dots and conjugated biomolecules such as phthalocyanine derivatives, porphyrin derivatives, hollow mesoporous silica nanoparticles (hMSN), and amino BODIPY derivative (BDPA). In some of the cases presented in Table 5, the QY of the proposed PSs is reduced compared with that of phthalocyanine derivatives. This indicates that the nano-PS has reduced fluorescence and thus reinforces the intersystem crossing to the triplet state. Intersystem crossing is enhanced as a result of the electron-donating nature of carbon-based quantum dots [122,123]. All the nano-PSs exhibit higher values of QY from CQDs/GrQDs when nano-PSs are excited where carbon-based quantum dots absorb. This observation is attributed to FRET and other processes related to fluorescence quenching of CQDs/GrQDs [122–124].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The role of singlet oxygen quantum yield for carbon-based quantum dots used in PDT
ce:section.ce:para: Singlet oxygen quantum yield (Φ1Ο2) is a measure for quantifying the singlet oxygen produced by the PS. This measurement indicates the ability of the PS to use light energy in order to convert molecular oxygen into singlet oxygen. Some of the presented studies on the PDT application of CQDs/GrQDs calculated the singlet oxygen quantum yield of the carbon-based quantum dots, biomolecules, or final nanoconjugates. Table 6 presents the results of the calculated Φ1Ο2 for carbon-based quantum dots conjugated with biomolecules such as phthalocyanine and porphyrine derivatives, BDPA, and hMSN. The conjugation of TMPP, ZnTMPP and GAClTMPP with carbon-based quantum dots improved the final singlet oxygen quantum yield. The increase in Φ1Ο2 of ZnTMPP and GaClTMPP conjugates compared with TMPP conjugates is attributed to the diamagnetic metals Zn and Ga, which have great photophysical characteristics [124]. The conjugation of GrQDs with hMSN resulted in lower singlet oxygen quantum yields compared with the GrQDs’ Φ1Ο2 [128,152]. In some cases, the addition of carbon-based quantum dots reduced the singlet oxygen quantum yield of the final PS. This result may originate from the screening effect of carbon-based quantum dots on biomolecules, which reduces the energy transfer to oxygen and thus decreases the quantum yield [123].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of carbon-based quantum dots with commonly used photosensitizers
ce:section.ce:para: The PDT performance of carbon-based quantum dots as PS was compared with commonly used PSs such as Ce6, porphyrin, derivatives, phthalocyanine derivatives, etc. Carbon-based quantum dots were conjugated with that of commonly PS Ce6, and their PDT action was compared with that of free Ce6 in PDT treatment of cervical, lung, and skin cancer. In all cases, the nanoconjugates exhibited higher PDT efficiency of free Ce6. This performance could be attributed to higher cellular uptake of nanoconjugates compared with Ce6. In addition to this, Ce6 has low water solubility, and clusters are formed, resulting in reduced cellular uptake and fluorescence intensities in the examined cell lines. The enhanced fluorescence properties of the nanoconjugates can be utilized for diagnostic purposes. Also, PSs based on the conjugation of carbon-based quantum dots and Ce6 exhibited improved biocompatibility. In lung and skin cancer, the linkage of HA in the nanoagents played a vital role in the improved PDT action due to the high affinity of cancerous cells [150,155,164].

The PDT action of PPIX was also compared with that of carbon-based quantum dots or their conjugates. Carbon-based quantum dots exhibited better results than PPIX in terms of dark toxicity. In the case of cervical cancer, GrQDs showed concentration-dependent PDT performance, while PPIX exhibited no changes in cell viability with increased concentration. A stronger PDT effect was achieved by GrQDs regarding drug dose and reduced cell viability upon irradiation [138]. Furthermore, similar PDT performance with PPIX showed nano-PSs based on the conjugation of CQDs with PPIX for the treatment of a skin cancer cell line. In this case, a lower concentration of drug was needed when the nanoconjugate was used as a PS [166]. Nanoconjugates of CQDs with PPIX and carbon nitride showed enhanced PDT efficiency in hypoxic and normoxic environments in breast cancer treatment. In vivo comparison revealed superb tumor growth inhibition with only one injection as a result of successful tumor specificity and singlet oxygen production [132].

BODIPY is used as a PDT agent due to its superb quantum yield, simple modification, and increased photostability. However, poor water solubility limits its wide application in PDT, so conjugation with carbon-based quantum dots has been investigated. To be particular, the conjugates of BODIPY with carbon dots exhibited a higher PDT effect than BODIPY alone, as a lower survival rate of HeLa cells was reported in the case of conjugates. In addition to this, the higher phototoxic activity of conjugates was confirmed by flow cytometry analysis results in comparison with the same concentration of BODIPY [140]. The BODIPY derivative, BDPA, was also tested for breast cancer in comparison with its conjugates with GrQDs. The conjugates exhibited improved aqueous solubility, singlet oxygen production, and PDT efficiency. The last remark originates from the insignificant change in tumor growth inhibition for BDPA [135].

Phthalocyanine derivatives are commonly used 2nd generation PSs in PDT. Their low water solubility encourages their conjugation with water-soluble molecules such as carbon-based quantum dots. The conjugation of morpholine substituted phthalocyanines with GrQDs resulted in a lower singlet oxygen quantum yield and lower PDT efficiency compared with phthalocyanines alone. However, increased cellular uptake was reported for GrQDs-phthalocyanines conjugates [123]. On the other hand, conjugation of ZnTPPcQ with CQDs and GrQDs showed lower survival rates in HeLa cells in comparison with free phthalocyanine. In addition to this, nanoconjugates exhibited a red shift in absorbance and a higher singlet oxygen quantum yield compared with phthalocyanine alone [122].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of carbon-based quantum dots electrodes in immunosensors
ce:section.ce:para: Nanomaterials were widely applied for production of biosensors with enhanced electrocatalytic activity, selectivity, sensitivity, and reproducibility [182,183]. Biosensors consisting of nanomaterials have quite inherent advantages, such as robustness, easy miniaturization, excellent detection limits and ability to handle small analyte volumes [184]. The recent advances in nanotechnology have demonstrated great promise in cancer diagnostics by means of miniaturized biosensors for early and efficient diagnosis [185,186].In the most of the cases the development of precise biosensors for various applications in cancer detection and therapy was carried out upon modification of their surface with appropriate functional groups, targeting biomolecules, drugs, and genes [187]. Among the different classes of nanomaterials, the carbon-based materials characterized by the presence of graphene structure, such as graphene quantum dots (GrQDs) demonstrate numerous advantages due to their outstanding chemical, physical, and optical properties, that lead to ultrasensitive and selective biosensor devices. The high performance of the GrQDs-based electrochemical biosensors is related to their large surface area, their excellent biocompatibility, their quantum confinement and edge effects, their abundant sites for chemical modification, as well as their high conductivity [188]. Consequently, GrQDs were widely applied in electrochemistry as electrode materials in the field of electrochemical biosensing for early detection of cancer biomarkers associated with various types of cancer [189,190]. Biosensors for cancer diagnosis are designed to recognize and convert into signal, specific cancer analytes, such as antibodies, proteins, carcinoma antigens, nucleic acids, cancer related byproducts such as H2O2, proteins exposed on the surface of cancer cells and changes in pH [191,192]. Recently, different electrochemical sensors based on GrQDs have been developed for the early, fast and sensitive diagnosis of cancer. For instance, a hybrid nanomaterial consisting of Graphene Quantum Dots (GrQDs) and multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) was successfully applied by Serafín et al. [193] as nanocarriers for the determination of IL-13 receptor α2 in numerous patients diagnosed with colorectal cancer at different stages. The lower limit of detection of 0,8 ng/mL estimated by means of amperometry technique exhibits the great electrocatalytic performance of fabricated GrQDs/MWCNTs sensor and the importance of using GrQDs for improving the response of electrochemical sensors. Serafin et al. [194] determined also simultaneously the metastasis-related biomarkers IL-13Rα2 and CDH-17 using similar hybrid nanomaterial consisting of graphene quantum dots (GrQDs) and multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs). The proposed biosensor allows the selective analysis of both biomarkers with LOD values of 1,4 (IL-13sRα2) and 0,03 ng/mL (CDH-17). In addition, Centane and Nyokong [195] fabricated a novel electrochemical sensor consisting of sulfur/nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (SN-GrQDs) for the detection of the breast cancer biomarker human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2). Namely, in this research work a traditional glassy carbon electrode (GCE) modified with sulfur/nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (SN-GrQDs), polypyrrole (PPy), and cobalt phthalocyanine (CoPc) exhibited enhanced response towards electrochemical detection of HER2 with lower limit of detection of 1,41 p.m./mL (estimated by means of electrochemical impedance spectroscopy technique). From this work it arises that the GCE/SNGrQDs/PPy/CoPc sensor has considerable potential for application in the early detection of HER2 as breast cancer biomarker. Furthermore, Ilies et al. [196] fabricated a novel type of paste electrode consisting of graphene quantum dots, and carbon nanotubes (GrQDs/CNTs) along with paraffin. This sensor has been successfully applied for detection of the anti-cancer chemotherapy drug doxorubicine (DOX), that is used for the treatment of various forms of sarcoma and cancer, including bladder cancer, breast cancer, leukemia, liver cancer, head and neck cancer, as well as lung cancer. The fabricated GrQDs-based paste electrode demonstrated enhanced electrochemical response towards DOX with lower limit of detection of 1.0 ng/L (estimated by means of differential pulse voltammetry technique). Besides, Pothipor et al. [197] suggested the biosensor consisted of graphene quantum dots along with graphene oxide and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs/GrQDs/GO) for the detection of breast cancer clinically related microRNAs biomarkers(miRNA-21, miRNA-155, miRNA-210). The fabricated biosensor exhibited a great electrocatalytic performance with lower limits of detection of 0,04, 0,33, and 0,28 fM for miRNA-21, miRNA-155, and miRNA-210, respectively. These findings demonstrate that the fabricated biosensor could improve diagnostic accuracy for early breast cancer detection. Additionally, Yang et al. [198] fabricated an ultrasensitive electrochemical immunosensor consisting of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GrQDs), PtPd bimetallic nanoparticles, and Au nanoparticles for the analysis of carcino-embryonic antigen (CEA). This immunosensor demonstrate low detection limit (2 fg/mL), high sensitivity, good reproducibility, long-term stability, and acceptable selectivity. Quantitative detection of carcino-embryonic antigen (CEA) was carried out also by Ganganboina and Doong [199]. Namely, gold-polyaniline (Au-PANI) nanowires decorated with nitrogen/sulfur doped graphene quantum dots (NS-GrQDs) was successfully applied for the detection of CEA (lower limit of detection 0,01 ng/mL) in human serum samples with excellent recovery.

In summary, this review presented the main principles of PDT and carbon-based quantum dots, while numerous recently synthesized PSs based on CQDs and GrQDs were also presented. In order to deal with the disadvantages of other commonly used therapeutic modalities, PDT is being investigated as a cancer therapy. However, a lot of limitations of PSs prevent their extensive clinical applications in cancer treatment. Nanotechnology constitutes a popular trend in PDT research due to the appearance of new material properties at the nanoscale. Carbon nanomaterials, such as CQDs and GrQDs, are new types of carbon allotropes, that fulfill numerous criteria of an ideal PS, such as non-toxicity, constant composition, simply synthesis, and the exhibition of strong photoluminescence. Recent studies proved that the application of carbon-based quantum dots in PDT improves selectivity, cytotoxicity, and secures delivery of hydrophobic molecules to target tissue. The use of carbon nanomaterials in the synthesis of PS drugs can be achieved by applying known PSs as conjugating agents or precursor compounds. A widespread technique involves the formation of composites consisting of carbon-based quantum dots and targeting biomolecules, such as folic acid, to improve selectivity for cancer cells. In recent research work CQDs and GrQDs were utilized for the successful deliver of chemotherapeutic drugs, including DOX and MTX. The high levels of intracellular H2O2 in tumor cells was the main focus of research in some recently published studies. Specifically, in these studies metal ions were conjugated with PSs based on carbon-based quantum dots for the production of vital components for PDT. Although this review article discusses some promising studies for application of CQDs and GrQDs as potential PSs to treat various types of cancer, further research has to be carried out especially in the field of clinical applications. Confidently, in vivo experiments can reveal new insights in the development of PSs. In addition, further modification of tested PSs based on carbon dots concerning synthesis, precursors, and doping agents has to be done.

Alexandra Karagianni: Conceptualization, Writing - Original draft preparation, Nikos G. Tsierkezos: Writing, Reviewing & Editing, Maurizio Prato: Reviewing & Editing, Mauricio Terrones: Reviewing & Editing, Konstantinos V. Kordatos: Conceptualization, Supervision, Reviewing & Editing.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Alexandra Karagianni is currently a PhD candidate in the field of nanotechnology in the School of Chemical Engineering, National Technical University of Athens (NTUA). She received her Integrated Master in Chemical Engineering from NTUA in 2021. Her research interests are mainly focused on the synthesis and characterization of carbon allotropes such as carbon quantum dots and nanohybrid materials based on the conjugation of carbon quantum dots with photo-related compounds. In addition, she performs research on application of carbon quantum dots and their nanohybrid materials in photoinduced processes, including photodynamic therapy and photocatalysis.

Nikos G. Tsierkezos was born in Cyprus. He completed his studies in Chemistry at the National Kapodistrian University of Athens (1996) and afterwards he performed at the same University in the laboratory of Physical Chemistry his research work to receive the Master of Science Degree (1999) and the Ph.D. Degree (2002). He worked as academic researcher at the Technical University of Berlin (2002-2004), Humboldt University of Berlin (2004-2007), and in Institute of Organic Chemistry and Biochemistry of Academy of Sciences of Czech Republic in Prague (2007-2008). In September 2008 he started to perform his independent research work in the field of synthesis and electrochemistry on carbon nanomaterials at the Technical University Ilmenau in Germany, in order to receive the academic degree of Habilitation (2016). In 2016 he became Privatdozent (Associate Professor) at the Technical University Ilmenau. His research interests are focused on production and investigation of novel electrochemical sensors based on carbon nanomaterials, with particular emphasis their modification and their application in biosensing. His scientific achievements are documented in more than 100 publications in peer-reviewed journals.

Maurizio Prato is Professor of Organic Chemistry at the University of Trieste and Ikerbasque Research Professor at CIC biomaGUNE, Spain. He is member of the Italian Academy of Sciences (Accademia Nazionale dei Lincei), the Real Spanish Academy of Sciences, and the National Academy of Inventors (USA). His research focuses on the synthesis of novel functional materials, for applications in materials science and nanomedicine, which include spinal cord repair, splitting of water, and reduction of carbon dioxide into useful chemicals.

Mauricio Terrones, obtained his B.Sc. degree in Engineering Physics with first class honors at Universidad Iberoamericana, in Engineering Physics in 1992. He received his D.Phil. degree from University of Sussex in 1998 under the supervision of Sir Prof. Harold W. Kroto (Nobel Laureate, FRS). He has co-authored more than 650 publications in international journals, and counts with more than 76,000 citations to his work (His H index is 121; Google Scholar H=134). He has received numerous national and international recognitions. He is member of the Mexican Academy of Sciences since 2002, fellow of the American Association for the Advancement of Science (AAAS), the Royal Society of Chemistry (UK), the American Physical Society (APS) and the Academy of Sciences for the Developing World (TWAS). Since 2017, he has been recognized as a Highly Cited Researcher in Physics and Materials Science, being among the top 1% most cited works according to the Web of Science and Clarivate Analytics. Mauricio is the Editor-in-Chief of the journal Carbon (IF=11.307), an Evan Pugh University Professor, The Verne M. Willaman Professor of Physics at Penn State University. He works on low dimensional materials that mainly involve 1- and 2-Dimensions, ranging from carbon nanotubes and graphene nanoribbons to graphene, boron nitride and chalcogenide monolayers.

Konstantinos V. Kordatos is Professor of Chemistry at the National Technical University of Athens, Greece. He received his Bachelor Degree in Chemistry at the University of Crete, Greece (1993). He obtained his Ph.D. in the Fullerene group at the University of Sussex, UK (1999). He worked as academic researcher scientist at the Fundamental Research Laboratories, NEC Corporation, Tsukuba, Japan (1996) as well as at the Institute of Polymers and Organic Solids, University of Santa Barbara, California, USA (1997). He worked as a postdoctoral fellow in the research group of Prof. Maurizio Prato at the University of Trieste, Italy (1998-2001). His research interest is focused on synthesis, characterization, and application of carbon-based nanomaterials in areas such as Energy and Environment. Furthermore, the utilization of agricultural by-products (bio-wastes) for the synthesis of advanced inorganic materials is also part of his research interest. His research work (more than 70 scientific articles) has been published in peer reviewed journals.
